coredata.dc:title: The dichotomy of nanotechnology as the cutting edge of agriculture: Nano-farming as an asset versus nanotoxicity
coredata.dc:creator: Behl, Tapan
Kaur, Ishnoor
Sehgal, Aayush
Singh, Sukhbir
Sharma, Neelam
Bhatia, Saurabh
Al-Harrasi, Ahmed
Bungau, Simona
coredata.dc:description: The unprecedented setbacks and environmental complications, faced by global agro-farming industry, have led to the advent of nanotechnology in agriculture, which has been recognized as a novel and innovative approach in development of sustainable farming practices. The agricultural regimen is the “head honcho” of the world, however presently certain approaches have been imposing grave danger to the environment and human civilization. The nano-farming paradigm has successfully elevated the growth and development of plants, parallel to the production, quality, germination/transpiration index, photosynthetic machinery, genetic progression, and so on. This has optimized the traditional farming into precision farming, utilising nano-based sensors and nanobionics, smart delivery tools, nanotech facets in plant disease management, nanofertilizers, enhancement of plant adaptive potential to external stress, role in bioenergy conservation and so on. These applications portray nanorevolution as “the big cheese” of global agriculture, mitigating the bottlenecks of conventional practices. Besides the applications of nanotechnology, the review identifies the limitations, like possible harmful impact on environment, mankind and plants, as the “Achilles heel” in agro-industry, aiming to establish its defined role in agriculture, while simultaneously considering the risks, in order to resolve them, thus abiding by “technology-yes, but safety-must”. The authors aim to provide a significant opportunity to the nanotech researchers, Botanists and environmentalists, to promote judicial use of nanoparticles and establish a secure and safe environment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Behaviour of Nanoparticles (NPs) in the Soil System. NPs are subjected to chemical, biological and/or physical changes, when they enter the soil. Aggregation is the most important physical alteration, which curbs the NP surface area, reduces the mobility of porous media thereby affecting their reactivity. Two forms of aggregation exist, homo-aggregation (aggregation of two or more similar NPs) and hetero-aggregation (aggregation of NPs with another particle in environment. Some NPs are taken up by the plant roots, while other undergo degradation.

Applications of nanotechnology components in multiple aspects of agriculture: crop protection (nanopesticides), crop improvement (nanobiotechnology), precision farming (nanosensors), plant adaptation (nanoparticles), growth and development (nanofertilizers).

Destructive effects of nanoparticles, depleting plant growth and development. NPs disrupt the cell membrane and the components of the cell, via induction of oxidative stress. The photosynthetic process, regulated by the mitochondria, is impaired, resulting in changes in the genetic profile. Also, NPs generate a negative impact on germination, which mitigates pollen viability, reduces biomass production, alters absorption profile of water and nutrients, as well as decreasing the number and area of plant leaves, therefore, abbreviating plant growth.
Elevated ROS as a significant impact of nanomaterials in living beings, resulting in altered functioning and cell death.
Delivery of plant fertilizers by different nano-formulations.

head.ce:title: The dichotomy of nanotechnology as the cutting edge of agriculture: Nano-farming as an asset versus nanotoxicity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The unprecedented setbacks and environmental complications, faced by global agro-farming industry, have led to the advent of nanotechnology in agriculture, which has been recognized as a novel and innovative approach in development of sustainable farming practices. The agricultural regimen is the “head honcho” of the world, however presently certain approaches have been imposing grave danger to the environment and human civilization. The nano-farming paradigm has successfully elevated the growth and development of plants, parallel to the production, quality, germination/transpiration index, photosynthetic machinery, genetic progression, and so on. This has optimized the traditional farming into precision farming, utilising nano-based sensors and nanobionics, smart delivery tools, nanotech facets in plant disease management, nanofertilizers, enhancement of plant adaptive potential to external stress, role in bioenergy conservation and so on. These applications portray nanorevolution as “the big cheese” of global agriculture, mitigating the bottlenecks of conventional practices. Besides the applications of nanotechnology, the review identifies the limitations, like possible harmful impact on environment, mankind and plants, as the “Achilles heel” in agro-industry, aiming to establish its defined role in agriculture, while simultaneously considering the risks, in order to resolve them, thus abiding by “technology-yes, but safety-must”. The authors aim to provide a significant opportunity to the nanotech researchers, Botanists and environmentalists, to promote judicial use of nanoparticles and establish a secure and safe environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: A sustainable and reliable agriculture is a fundamental aspect of accomplishing “zero hunger”, that is one of the 17 project aims of the United Nations (UN) (Usman et al., 2020). The present farming habits associated with the green revolution have greatly contributed to the food supply across the globe. However, it has simultaneously exerted unintentional and detrimental impact on the ecosystem, which creates a dire need for the establishment of more innovative and eco-friendly farming approaches. The prime issues related to the agricultural practices, include decreased productivity, increasing population, alleviated level of nutrients, limited availability of water and agricultural lands, climatic changes, retarded soil organic matter, low efficiency of nutrient use, elevated pesticide residues, limited supply of skilled labourers, lack of awareness among the general farmers as well as environmental issues (Al-Shayaa et al., 2021). Unethical use of pesticides and fertilizers has been elevated in the agricultural field, which has further enhanced the level of toxic agrochemicals (Gowri and Thangaraj, 2020), not only on the surface water, but also in the ground water, on account of poor awareness among the general farmers. This, in turn, has increased the cost of purification of water and health facilities, which has alleviated viability of aquatic life. Also, the agricultural practices, which diminish the quality of the soil, have led to eutrophication (overenrichment in water bodies) (Serediak et al., 2014), which has elevated the overall costs. The use of excessive pesticides has also destroyed the useful insects and microbes (Lo, 2010). Alongside, lowering of groundwater levels in places, where agricultural practices are using groundwater in excess for irrigation purposes. Collectively, resulting in degradation if ecosystem, thereby posing a great threat to human civilization (Acharya and Pal, 2020).

The nanomaterials (NMs) are nano-structured materials have been categorized into different ranges, depending upon their sizes and multiple dimensions (D), namely size 0 (0D), size 1 (1D), size 2 (2D) and size 3 (3-D) (Yu et al., 2015). Nanomaterial (NM) has been defined as the material with any external dimension in nanoscale or the surface or interna structure in nanoscale dimensions, while a nanoparticle (NP) is a nano-entity, with all three external dimensions in nanoscale, as per the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) (Boverhof et al., 2015). The nanomaterials (NMs) are described as natural or artificial materials, comprising of particles, as aggregates/agglomerates or occurring independently, where about 50% or more are in the number-size distribution, with the size ranging between 1 and 100 nm, as per the European Commission (2011).

Nanotechnology has exhibited an extensive range of applications in Pharmaceuticals, information technology, textile industry, renewable energy production, and so on. Nanotechnology provides an economical and environment-friendly approach in the management of agriculture. Nanotechnology in agriculture, aims to improve food values and nutrient content, retarded agricultural inputs, reduction of toxic chemicals and longer shelf life. This field comprises of important tools for detection of phytopathogens, packaging, food security, treatment of disease and potential delivery systems (Echiegu, 2017). Various materials, like metal oxides, silicates, ceramics, emulsions, polymers, lipids and plant extracts are used in the development and coating of NMs (Ruttkay-Nedeckey et al., 2017). Richard P. Feynman laid the basis of nanotechnology and Norio Taniguchi coined this term “Nanotechnology, in 1974 (Rai et al., 2018). Thereafter, it has been identified as a cutting-edge technology, not only in agriculture, but in all the areas development. Their use is attributed to increased efficiency, large surface area, ease of functionalization, appreciable electrical and optical properties, high stability and adsorption ability, diversity in functions and availability of active sites on the surface (Kumar et al., 2016; Xiao et al., 2016; Kumar et al., 2017a, b). Rapid progression of nanotechnology has, besides the benefits, has also exerted potential negative effects, by entering in the food web (Rizwan et al., 2017), where the detrimental impact can be transferred to the air, soil and water ecosystems, which creates a strong demand for a comprehensive discussion on nanotoxicity.

The manuscript attempts to establish a relationship between the dichotomous role of nanoparticles (NPs) and agriculture, thereby spreading awareness about the use of these particles and balancing their role. Parallel to the applications of this innovative field in agriculture, the review also provides a detailed overview of the phytotoxic effects, human health damaging effects, biotic toxicity and environmental issues associated with nanoparticle (NP) use. The authors have compiled the last ten-year data from multiple publications and databases, focussing on the areas concerned with the role of nanoparticles in agricultural advancement, associated with potential risks in their application, on environment and living beings, which can be evaluated and solved. The manuscript aims to formulate a unique reference for the global researchers, from varying phases of Botany and agriculture, to provide an opportunity and fundamental prospects for the future, in order to establish the practice of nanotechnology-based approaches in agriculture and food security.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The basis of nanotechnology in agriculture: opening a new window at a global level
ce:section.ce:para: Agricultural has a significant impact on the globe, where many studies have focussed on studying the uses of various plants (Khan et al., 2019 Xiao et al., 2020). The agricultural business market has dropped and currently more than 400 global companies are actively participating in nanotechnology research and development, where this number is constantly increasing, expected to reach more than thousand in the upcoming decade (Duhan et al., 2017).

The harmful chemicals from pesticides have been collected in the ground and surface water. (Mahmood et al., 2015). As a result, the cost associated with water purification and minimizing the health impacts has increased tremendously. Furthermore, not only land, but the excessive use of chemicals has also retarded the viability of the marine organisms. Also, soil degrading agricultural practices have led to increased eutrophication, which therefore increased the overall costs, and impose danger on the insects, microbes and wild organisms. The agriculture-prone areas have imposed a significant threat to the groundwater levels, which has been constantly declining in these areas. Therefore, all these limitations and issues have contributed to ecosystem degradation, which has put the human civilization at risk (Foley et al., 2011). Therefore, the agricultural and allied sectors aim to retard such limitations, and feed the global population, with minimum impact on the mother earth, simultaneously maintaining a reliable and sustainable practice of agriculture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The behaviour of nanomaterials in soil
ce:section.ce:para: The NMs partition out in the aqueous and solid phases of the soil system, as a result of their interaction with the natural colloids, mineral components and organic fractions (Darlington et al., 2009; Ben-Moshe et al., 2010). Most of the research related to NMs has been carried out in water systems, thereby minimizing the data available for the fate of NMs in the soil system. Primarily, the studies related to soil suspensions have supplied most of the information related to the behaviour of NMs in the soil systems. Numerous chemical, physical as well as biological changes are undergone by NMs, when they enter the soil, based upon the nature of these particles and their interaction with multiple inorganic and organic components of the soil (Usman et al., 2020). When these particles enter into the soil, the major physical process, which takes place is aggregation, that minimizes the surface area available for NPs, thereby affecting their reactivity. Furthermore, elevated aggregate size will alleviate the ability of NMs in the porous surface, thus influencing the behaviour and reactivity of NMs (Lowry et al., 2012). Aggregation exists in two different forms, namely homoaggregation, i.e., aggregation between similar NMs, and the other one being heteroaggregation, which unlike homoaggregation, means aggregation between a NP and another particle existing in the same environment, such as natural colloids (Fig. 1 ) (Lowry et al., 2012; Batley et al., 2013). Due to enhanced concentration levels of other particles in the environment, heteroaggregation holds greater significance than homoaggregation (Lowry et al., 2012; Cornelis et al., 2014). Heteroaggregation mainly occurs between a pH range of point of zero charge (PZC) of natural colloid and NPs (Lowry et al., 2012; Cornelis et al., 2014), where non-availability of electrostatic repulsion at this region, as well as opposite surface charges on NMs and natural colloids aids the heteroaggregation process (Kim et al., 2012). However, homoaggregation takes place with the use of greater concentration of titanium dioxide (TiO2) NPs, versus kaolin clay, but the proportion of homoaggregation to heteroaggregation, due to the availability of phosphate or Escherichia coli (Xu et al., 2020). Soil colloids were reported to function as carriers of zinc oxide (ZnO) NPs, which are strongly absorbed. The aggregates of these NPs were related to the soil-clay minerals by electron microprobe (Zhao et al., 2012). Thus, in a natural environment, the fate of NMs is more likely to be regulated by heteroaggregation. Even though the role of heteroaggregation is crystal clear, most of the investigations target homoaggregation (Usman et al., 2020). Furthermore, heteroaggregation has been studied with greater level of NMs, during the availability of limited concentration of natural colloids (Batley et al., 2013). In case of NMs, soil colloids and minerals, mainly iron and clay minerals are recognized to be fundamental. The significance of soil colloid was portrayed as a result of quantification of concentration of NMs in varying particle size soil fractions, targeting the colloids, that are water-dispersible and retained greater concentration of MWCNTs (Zhang et al., 2017) and silver (Ag) NPs (Makselon et al., 2018). The impact of soil clay minerals, montmorillonite and kaolinite were investigated, on the stability of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), stabilized by surfactants, which were deposited in the presence of these minerals. Also, the movement of MWCNTs in the soil was reported to be blocked by the presence of these minerals, which also affected the stability of these nanotubes (Han et al., 2008). The TiO2 NPs were reported to form aggregates with kaolin, according to a recent study conducted (Xu et al., 2020). However, the porous media was found to be elevated, as a result of movement of these aggregates, in the presence of Escherichia coli and phosphates, on account of alterations in the state of aggregation and elevation in repulsive interactions with the porous material (Usman et al., 2020). Another significant parameter is soil organic matter (SOM), which exerts a significant impact on the behaviour and fate of NMs, via stabilization and absorption (Lei et al., 2018). Surface coverage of NMs takes place as a result of SOM adsorption, thus alleviating the active surface area, which curbs the significant effects of NMs (Usman et al., 2020). Moreover, contradictory actions may be exhibited by SOM on the stability and movement characteristics of NMs, based upon their nature. SOM has been reported to enhance NM aggregation, via flocculation. However, it can the stability characteristics of NMs, by enhancing the electrostatic stability (Philippe and Schaumann, 2014; Klitzke et al., 2015). SOM has also been reported to regulate the solubility of nano-oxide NPs, which has been evidently portrayed in recent investigation, depicting elevation in SOP, and thus enhanced solubility of copper oxide (CuO) NMs (Gao et al., 2019). The aggregation of MWCNTs, was found to be prevented via steric hindrance and electrostatic repulsion, in the presence of sodium in <than 4 nM (nM) concentration or in solutions of pH (equal to or greater than 4) (Usman et al., 2020). The stability and movement of TiO2 NPs were found to be affected by iron minerals and content of SOM (Zhang et al., 2019). Also, organic matter might aid in inducing negative charge to the NMs, thereby electrostatic destabilization of the positive NMs, under environmental Ph (Philippe and Schaumann, 2014; Lei et al., 2018).

The fate of NPs is influenced by principles of solution chemistry and nature of fractions of organic matter (Ghosh et al., 2008). The stability of aluminium oxide NPs was reported to be elevated due to availability of humic acids at the pH of PZC or above it. However, in the presence of humic acids, the acidic conditions promoted strong aggregation (Ghosh et al., 2008). At higher concentration levels of NPs, the silver NPs depicted greater stability in the presence of organic matter, which portrayed the concentration dependent nature of the stabilizing effect (Klitzke et al., 2015). Besides the total SOM content, the heterogeneity of fractions of SOM should also be considered according to recent investigations (Xu et al., 2019), in which the impact of two major SOM fractions on hydroxyapatite NP movement, was compared which was blocked by organic matter, related to the minerals, due to mechanical staining and deposition on the surface (Usman et al., 2020). On the contrary, the movement of hydroxyapatite NPs was found to be improved by dissolved organic matter, due to elevation in electrostatic repulsion among these NPs and surfaces of the soil (Xu et al., 2019). Various abiotic and biotic processes regulate the chemical changes in the NMs, and comprise of reduction, oxidation, resolution, degradation, modification and sulfidation of the surface (Lei et al., 2018; Lowry et al., 2012). Numerous NMs comprise of redox-sensitive components, such as iron and silver. The structural ferrous (Fe2+) is alleviated and the stoichiometry is altered as a result of oxidation of mix valent (Fe2+ and Fe3+) minerals (Usman et al., 2018). The reactivity of these mixed valent iron minerals for remediation of environment is significantly affected by the stoichiometry of such minerals (Usman et al., 2018). On account of limited solubility profile of silver sulfide, which influences their occurrence in the environment, the toxicity of Ag NPs was alleviated as a result of sulfidation of NMs (Levard et al., 2012). The retention and mobility of NMs is also affected by the surface coating of such particles (He et al., 2019). For example, the mobility of Ag NMs was elevated after coating it with polyvinyl pyrrolidone and citrate (He et al., 2019). Similarly, magnetite coated dimercaptosuccinic acid depicted varying mobility characteristics (Al -Sid -Cheikh et al., 2019) However, future studies are required in order to promote better and reliable assessment related to the effect of NM coating on their behaviour and fate in soil, along with their efficacy. Fig. 1 demonstrates the behaviour of NPs in the soil, pictorially (Usman et al., 2020).

The fate of NPs in soil is a significant consideration, in studying the impact of NP application on the plants, as plants themselves are the part of the soil ecosystem, which makes it crucial to investigate the physical, chemical and biological alterations in NPs, upon reaching the soil particles. It could be beneficial in determining any negative impact on the living beings in the soil, as well as on the fertility of the land. It is significant to understand the flow of NPs through the plant, which has been discussed earlier. Some NPs are taken up by the plant roots, while others are degraded. Aggregation has been marked as an essential physical change, which either promotes aggregation of two or more NPs, or NPs with other particles in the environment, which can impede the mobility of porous media, thereby hindering their reactivity. Also, certain NPs might enter the groundwater. Therefore, all these possibilities should be considered, while applying nanoformulations to plants. As the article not only targets the applications of NPs in promoting plant growth and development, but also focusses on the resultant impact on the environment, the effect of NPs and their fate in the soil appears to be a major approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Managerial facets of nano-agriculture
ce:section.ce:para: Multiple areas of legislation have directly or indirectly covered the NMs. The NMs have been required to be included into varying regulations of consumers and products, as per the European Union (EU) legislation (Brinch et al., 2016). Other legislative bodies have also been formed to aid in better implementation of NMs, such as the Annexes to the Registration, Evaluation, Authorization and Restriction of Chemicals (REACH) Regulation (European Commission, 2012) and Novel Food Regulation (European Commission, 2013). The regulatory solutions, related to the production of food and feed, vary internationally, where specific nano-related provisions have been found to be introduced by countries like, Switzerland and EU, in the legislation (Amenta et al., 2015). A mutual and collaborative conduct is required by the international countries, in order to promote effective dissemination of information and increase the awareness among the general farmers (Amenta et al., 2015). Moreover, there are certain areas, where an immediate action is required, primarily, introduction of acts to facilitate assessment and evaluation pesticide hazards; provisions to notably provide a clear definition of NMs; risk assessment of the food and feed production and labelling and reporting schemes of NPs (Acharya and Pal, 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanorevolution: the big cheese of agriculture
ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology has emerged out as a significant asset to the agricultural sector, where it has the ability to reform the existing farming practices. NMs exhibit potential applications in the agricultural regimen to elevate the yield and productivity of the crop, along with improvement in soil health, as discussed in this section, that has highlighted the significance of nanotechnology in precision farming and nanobiosensor technology, NMs as delivery tools, NPs to augment plant adaptation to external stresses, nanofertilizers, NMs in plant disease management and nanopesticides, as well as role of NMs in enhancing plant growth and development, seed germination, crop growth and quality enrichment. Fig. 2 depicts the potential applications of nanotechnology in agriculture, covering all the major areas discussed further in the review (Shang et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synergistic impact of precision farming and use of nanosenors/nanobionics in monitoring physical parameters
ce:section.ce:para: Precision farming facilitates development of wireless network systems to enable detection, assessment and regulation of agricultural activities, management of site-specific crop in particular, with an extensive variety of pre- and post-production facets of agriculture (Dwivedi et al., 2016). The recent progression of tissue engineering and targeted delivery of clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR) and associated protein, Cas (CRISPR-associated protein) mRNA (messenger ribo-nucleic acid) and single-guide RNA (sgRNA), based upon nanoparticles, to promote genetic modification of crops, is a notable achievement in the agricultural science (Ran et al., 2017; Miller et al., 2017a,b; Kim et al., 2017). Also, nanotechnology aims to provide efficient solutions to prevent numerous challenged associated with the environment, as evident by the production of nano-sensors, which enables detection of environmental stress and elevates the strength of plants against diseases (S.Y. Kwak et al., 2017). Therefore, this technology offers a reliable and sustainable solution to the agricultural problems, besides detecting them, thereby providing social and fair applications. Precision farming constitutes the process of escalating the crop yields and alleviating the use of herbicides, pesticides, insecticides, fertilizers and fungicides, via effective monitoring processes, and using remote sensing equipment, computational tools and global satellite positioning system to evaluate the environmental status, thereby favoring plant growth and recognizing associated issues (Soutter, 2021). Besides, precision farming also aids in regulate growth and development of plants, analyzing the condition of the soil, ensuring adequate use of fertilizers and chemicals and mitigating environmental pollution by reducing agricultural waste. The use of sensors and monitoring tools will positively influence the precision farming techniques in the future. The significance and use of global positioning system (GPS) based autonomous sensors has been elevated by nanotech-enables systems, which in turn has exhibited effective results in monitoring the growth of crops and status of soil (Soutter, 2021). Furthermore, the productivity in the field is also enhanced as a result of the use of smart sensor devices, associated with precision farming, which has enabled supply of necessary information to the farmers, assisting their decision-making abilities, related to the matters concerning growth and development of plants and quality of soil (Soutter, 2019). Ultimately, precision farming constitutes high-tech devices to enable measurement of environmental stress conditions and aid in determining the growth of plants, alongside recognition of the nature and location of problems, if any. Normalized difference in vegetative index (NDVI) sensor, also called ‘green seeker’, is a nanotech-based device, which comprises of diodes that produce red and near infrared light rays, to determine the NDVI values (Duhan et al., 2017). This device measures the normalized variation in the vegetative index, based upon the absorption of red light by chlorophyll in the plants, as a source of energy during the process of photosynthesis. Thus, healthy plants tend to absorb more amount of red light and reflect greater amount of near infrared light, thereby exhibiting higher NDVI value, which reverses in case of unhealthy plants (Duhan et al., 2017).
The equation for calculating NDVI is as follows:
NDVI = Near infrared light (reflected) – Red light (reflected)/near infrared light (reflected) + red light (reflected).
Moreover, this tiny device significantly indicates the biomass of the plant, and also identifies the nitrogen requirement of the plant (Kumar et al., 2016).

Sensors based upon quantum dots (QDs) – fluorescence resonance energy, have been created to promote identification of Phytoplasma aurantifolia-caused witches’ broom disease of lime, which has been reported to exhibit greater sensitivity, 100% specificity as well as 5 ca. P. aurantifolia/μL (Rad et al., 2012). QDs exhibit effective luminescence, impressive photostability, reliability and a small emission-spectra, which render it with greater potential as compared to organic fluorescent dyes (Duhan et al., 2017). Also, QDs have been reported to aid in identification of pathogens, responsible for diseases of the plant kingdom, such as role of sensors, based upon ODs-fluorescence resonance energy transfer, in detection of Keskin (Polymyxa betae), which is the vector for the transmission of beet necrotic yellow vein virus, that causes Rhizomania in sugar beet (Safarpour et al., 2012). The integration of acetylcholinesterase enzyme with the optical transducer of cadmium telluride semiconductor QDs, by employing layer-by-layer assembly method, is used in the detection of organophosphorus pesticides (Ops) in fruits and vegetables, which results in the development of a greatly sensitive biosensor, dependent upon the mechanism of enzyme inhibition, with limit od detection (LOD) of 4.47 × 10–12 M for parathion and 1.05 × 10–11 M for paraoxan, which have been identified to be much more effective than the convention technique of gas chromatography and amperometric biosensors (Zheng et al., 2011). Detection of methyl parathion (at picogram levels) is enabled by QD formulation of thiol-stabilized luminescent cadmium telluride (CdTe), on account of its unique optical characteristics (Chouhan et al., 2010). CdTe QDs are incorporated with nanosphere formulations of highly fluorescent silica and water soluble CdTe QDs, to form biosensors for measurement fir deltmethrin in vegetables and fruits (Ge et al., 2011). Semi-automatic electroplating of inert metallic compounds into templates with definite particle diameter, leads to the construction of nano-barcodes, which are used for detection and evaluation of genetic expressions (Duhan et al., 2017). Nanotechnology mediated progression of applications in biotechnology has enhanced the resistance of plants towards extreme environmental conditions. Economical and rapid detection and use of resources of plant gene characteristics is enabled by nanotechnology-based gene sequencing (Branton et al., 2008).

Precision farming will curb waste production in the future, along with introduction of eco-friendly approaches (Day, 2005) and acceleration of agricultural processes like optimized harvesting and plantation time, management of water use and other applications of agro-chemical importance. A funding of approximately $30.3 million has been provided to the Cambridge startup CiBo Technologies, to establish software platform models, to aid the simulation of different conditions in the environment of complex agricultural ecosystem globally, which can develop “virtual fields”, exhibiting outcomes as in the real world, simultaneously permitting the feedbacks of the users and ways to potentiate and reform the real agricultural fields (Acharya and Pal, 2020). This promotes reduction in the production of agricultural waste, elevation of the reliability of the users, use of economical approaches to enhance agricultural trends and improvement in time to market ratio (https://www.cibotechnologies.com/). Similarly, precision farming also involves establishment of other startup organizations, such as Michigan Startup Farmobile, Broomfield startup and so on (Acharya and Pal, 2020). Furthermore, agricultural robots are smart technological tools, which aid in the reduction of use of agrochemicals, thereby offering an intelligent solution to the problems arising from the uncontrollable use of chemicals in the field. Such robotic technologies have also been implemented for crop segmentation activities in the field, by various agricultural researchers, thereby upgrading plant specific treatments as well as selective robot-mediated weeding, which accomplishes the primary focus of precision farming (Acharya and Pal, 2020). Convolutional neural network (CNN) has been used to develop newer techniques for detection of joint stem and segmentation of crop-weed (Lottes et al., 2020), essential for plant-specific management. Further, autonomous systems have been created using large-scale datasets of field robots, to enable proper conduct of agriculture (Chebrolu et al., 2017, 2018, 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nano-sensors and nano-biosensors: NPs as sensing materials
ce:section.ce:para: Biosensors transform a biological signal into an electrical response, in order to analyze the quantitative data, by using a microprocessor. This device comprises of some pivotal components, like a living being or biomolecules (functioning as a bio-recognition unit), transducer and a readable signal (Neethirajan et al., 2018). Nanobiosensors permit easy and accurate sensing, high specificity and selectivity profiles, abbreviated time of detection and monitoring of real-time signals, by using small portable devices (Song et al., 2021). Furthermore, they can detect single as well as multiple analytes, which can be detrimental to living organism (Acharya and Pal, 2020). Fictionalization, immobilization as well as miniaturization are interesting characteristics of nanobiosensors, which combine biocomponents of transduction system, into a complex arrangement, to enhance the analytical potential of nanomaterials (Arduini et al., 2016). The prime advantages of nanobiosensors in plant science, involve pesticide detection, examination of condition of soil, identification of food-derived pathogens, determination of antibiotic resistance, crop stress, detection of toxic pollutants and heavy metals, determination of content of nutrients and quality of food, assessment of plant growth, recognition of drug residues, and so on (Tarafdar et al., 2013). Furthermore, detection of dicofol (organochlorine pesticide) can be done by fluorescent nanosensors (Walia and Acharya, 2014). The colorimetric sensor, based upon gold NPs was used in the detection of dimethoate, which is an organophosphorous pesticide (Dar et al., 2016).

GPS – based upon the nanosensor technology, has aided the real time monitoring of agricultural fields, post cultivation, throughout the season of growth, where these wireless networks control and analyze the mechanism of controlled release by nanocarriers using signals without wires, present in the cultivated fields (Shang et al., 2019). As a result, comprehensive monitoring and a real time analysis of the growth of crop and reliable data of superior quality, which enables the conduct of most sustainable management approaches, without rendering excessive dose of agricultural inputs (El-Beyrouthya and El Azzi, 2014). The efficiency of water use, has been elevated to the fullest by the use of automatic nanosensor-based irrigation system, which, in combination with real-time, also promotes the estimation of tension between soil particles and water, especially in the current situation of limited water availability (Fraceto et al., 2016). Also, a wireless nanosensor has been created in order to detect and predict the attack by insects, which can facilitate application of fertilizers and pesticides on time (Afsharinejad et al., 2016). An immunosensor, based upon gold NPs (Au NPs) has been developed and reported to identify the karnal bunt disease in wheat crop (Singh et al., 2010). The 1D nanofibers were developed using potassium niobate, which were reported to exhibit an interesting ability to sense humidity in the atmosphere, on account of large ration of surface area to volume (Ganeshkumar et al., 2016). The upconversion NPs was developed, based upon a dual-functional platform, regulated by a simple glutathione, free from label, for fluorescence detection of the activity of acetylcholinesterase as well as toxicity profile of cadmium ions in water samples (Fang et al., 2017). Furthermore, in order to monitor and quantify minute concentrations of pollutants and pesticides, photosystem 2, comprising of nanosensors, has been developed, which enables accurate, economical and easy identification of organic pollutants and pesticides, prior to their disposal into the environment of the agricultural field (Panpatte et al., 2016). Varying types of nanosensors have been tested and evaluated in plants, like nanosenors based upon fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET); plasmonic nanosensors; nanowire nanosensors; electrochemical nanosensors based upon carbon as well as antibody nanosensors (Sanzari et al., 2019). Sucrose detection has been enabled by the development of a nanosensor, based upon fluorometric optical onion membrane, created by incorporation of invertase nano-gold clusters in membranes of plants (Bagal Kestwal et al., 2015). Identification and quantification of phytoalexins has been promoted by polystyrene nanoparticles, conjugated with fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) probes (Dumbrepatil et al., 2010). Functioning as deoxyribo-nucleic acid (DNA) biochemical labels, gold NPs have been reported to aid in the detection of Pseudomonas syringae and Arabidopsis thaliana, via differential pulse voltammetry on carbon electrodes, printed on disposable screens (Lau et al., 2017). Furthermore, mycotoxin can be detected with the help of smart nanosensors, such as 4mycosensor, which is an assay, based upon competitive antibody, which has been reported to indicate the presence of T-2/HT-2, FB1/FB2, ZEA and DON mycotoxins in wheat, corn, barley and oat (Lattanzio and Mivarlet, 2017). Surface functionalized carbon nanotubes were used to develop organophosphorous pesticide biosensor, which was highly sensitive. These nanotubes were supplemented with amino groups, and was used to regulate the effective immobilization of acetylcholinesterase on carbon electrode surface, to directly evaluate samples of vegetables (Yu et al., 2015). Organophosphate pesticides were also detected by using multiwall carbon nanotube-based acetyl cholinesterase biosensor, onto liposomal bioreactors (Yan et al., 2013). Gold nanospheres (limit of detection – 0.06 μg/dm3 for chlorpyrifos; 0.08 μg/dm3 for carbofuran) were used to develop an acetylcholinesterase biosensor, that was highly sensitive (Sun et al., 2013). Acetylcholinesterase, immobilized by zinc and selenium quantum dots, was used to detect organophosphate pesticides, with the help of an electrode, modified by graphene-chitosan nanocomposite, where methyl parathion was used as the inhibitor of model enzyme (Dong et al., 2013). Vertical assemblage of carbon nanotubes, wrapped with ssDNA, were used to precipitate polyaniline, on which acetylcholinesterase was immobilized, resulting in the construction of an electrochemical biosensor, for determining pesticides, namely, chlorpyrifos and methyl parathion. The enzymatic reaction of acetylcholinesterase and acetylcholine was hindered, in order to determine the pesticides, which induced minor pH alterations in the electrode surface surroundings (Viswanathan et al., 2009). The pollutants existing in the agricultural field and their effects can be monitored by using nanosensors, based upon single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) along with metallic NPs or nanotubes for gases (Sekhon, 2014). High sensitivity has been observed in the detection of organophosphorous agents, using gold NPs, via fluorescence spectroscopy (Dasary et al., 2008). Therefore, nanosensors have clearly an appreciable role to play in establishing sustainable agriculture, however, more extensive research in this field is required to completely establish its role in the agricultural industry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanobionics aid in agriculture
ce:section.ce:para: Plants like spinach, peanut and soybean, have been reported with elevated physiological aspects, like increased ability of photosynthesis and metabolism of nitrogen, by employing nanomaterials, based upon metals (Razzaq et al., 2015). Similarly, many carbon and metal-associated NPs have been significantly investigated and exploited for their role in plant growth and development. Nanobionics is the science of investigation and exploration of the electronic interactions in living systems, by employing NPs to elevate the cellular functions of the plant (Mali et al., 2020). This field is accelerating in the field of nanotechnology, and is attracting a great deal of attention among the botanists. NPs interact with the cell organelles of plants to elevate the local cellular functions, resulting in the advent of nano-bioengineering. Numerous studies have reported elevation in the photosynthetic process by employing single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) (Mali et al., 2020). Similarly, the absorption of solar energy has been enhanced by plasmon resonance of metal NPs, resulting in acceleration of carbon fixation (Giraldo et al., 2014). Furthermore, bionic plants have also been developed, as a significant component of nanobiotechnology, which are formed by incorporating NPs into the cell chloroplasts of living plants, to promote imaging and sensing of environmental objects, as well as enable interaction, either as infrared devices or self-powering of plants, as significant source of light, thus exhibiting appreciable role in precision farming (S.-Y. Kwak et al., 2017). On inserting single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) into light-adapted chloroplasts, the rate of electron transfer was found to be elevated by 49%, by supplementing light-mediated absorption, under in-vivo conditions. The results also indicated the role of SWCNTs in light-harvesting capacity near infrared fluorescence by retarding ROS production (which causes cell damage by promoting oxidation of lipids, DNA and proteins) (Rong et al., 2021) in chloroplast and could affect the process of sensing in plants, leading to elevated effectiveness of photosynthetic machinery as well as plant quantum yield (Shang et al., 2019). Nanoparticle formulations like, titanium dioxide NPs (TiO2 NPs), Silicon dioxide NPs (SiO2 NPs) and nanoceria (cerium oxide NPs), exhibit significant roles in elevation of photosynthesis and augmentation of photosynthetic machinery. TiO2 NPs promote carbon assimilation, RuBisCo activation and increased tolerance of flax plants towards drought conditions (Aghdam et al., 2016; Sharma and Kar, 2019). SiO2 NPs improve the activity of carbonic anhydrase and production of photosynthetic pigments, resulting in increased rate of photosynthesis (Siddiqui and Al-Whaibi, 2015). Nanoceria exhibits anti-oxidative effect, with ROS-scavenging potential, accelerating photosynthesis in Arabidopsis thaliana (Wu et al., 2017; Sharma and Kar, 2019). Thus, progression in the field of nanobionics in environmental assessment and improvement in crop production, has exposed a novel approach into the study of nanomaterial (NM) hybrids of functional plants (Giraldo et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Augmentation of delivery approaches: a novel frontier for nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Significant production in agricultural regime and precision farming requires optimum delivery of organic molecules and agrochemicals, along with transportation of oligonucleotides and DNA molecules into the plant cells (Joga et al., 2016). These organic substances are conventionally applied by broadcasting or spraying on to the crops, by which the concentration of chemicals able to reach the target areas of the plant are far less than the minimum effective concentration (MEC), essential for the optimum growth of the plan (Shang et al., 2019). Chemical degradation by hydrolysis or photolysis are major reasons for such delivery losses in agriculture (Yang et al., 2016). Nutrient bioavailability should be the prime area of concern in case of fertilizer delivery, due to soil chelation effects, microbial degradation, excessive use, run-off issues, hydrolysis and evaporation (Gogos et al., 2012). Progression in production of controlled release and slow-release pesticides, fertilizers, herbicides, fungicides etc., based upon nanotechnology approaches has attracted the agricultural researchers, in order to develop environment friendly practices (Panpatte et al., 2016). Nanotechnology is no more limited to the laboratory experiments and studies, but has been put to practical use in the agricultural fields. The controlled release of agrochemicals aims to promote periodic delivery of desired, calculated concentration of the agrochemicals, to facilitate complete positive biological impact and limited toxic or harmful outcomes (Shojaei et al., 2019a,b). On account of ease of attachment, large surface area as well as fast mass transfer, NPs enable effective delivery of agricultural chemicals (Ghormade et al., 2011). The release of potassium nitrate, as a fertilizer, was augmented by encapsulation with graphene oxide films, which was developed at economical costs (Zhang and Gao 2014). Also, the stability of agricultural chemicals was improved by NMs and they were protected from environmental degradation and release, resulting in elevated efficacy and alleviated quantity applied (Shang et al., 2019).

Nanotechnology has collaborated with biotechnology, to lead to the formulation of novel tools of molecular transporter, resulting in modification of genes and production of new organisms (Lyons, 2010). The nanobiotechnological components comprise of nanocapsules, nanofibers and NPs, to facilitate transportation of foreign DNA particles, facilitating modification of target genes. During the genetic material delivery, significant challenges are faced by the viral gene delivery vectors like limited size of the inserted genetic material, reduced host range, movement across the cell membrane, as well as nuclear trafficking (Ghormade et al., 2011). However, recent advancements in nanobiotechnology have developed significant opportunities to the global researchers to replace the genome of one organism with another (Torney et al., 2007). DNA fragment delivery is enabled by silicon dioxide NPs, to the target species, like corn and tobacco, without any adverse effects observed (Galbraith, 2007). Additionally, multiple varieties of insect-resistant crop were developed by delivery systems, assisted by NPs. For instance, in gene-gun technology, for cell/tissue bombardment, NPs coated with DNA function as bullets to facilitate transportation of desired genes to target species (Vijayakumar et al., 2010). The small interfering RNA (siRNA) delivery vehicles, incorporated in chitosan NPs, have led to improved crop production, and facilitated target specific pest control, on account of effective RNA binding potential and membrane permeation ability of chitosan (Zhang et al., 2010). Significant progression of delivery of CRISPR/Cas9 single guide RNA (sgRNA), which is a RNA-directed system of defense in prokaryotes and employed for genome editing in plants, has been a new awakening in the genetic engineering (Miller et al., 2017a,b). However, limited efficiency of delivery is still an obstacle, which is yet to be eliminated. Furthermore, it has been reported that the positively-charged arginine gold NPs - Cas9En (E-tag)-RNP-mediated delivery of sgRNA exhibits 30% efficacy in gene editing potential of nucleus or cytoplasm in cultured cell lines, which would significantly influence the crop development research in the future (Mout et al., 2017). In the current agricultural era, where the use of pesticides has faced multiple regulatory restrictions, on account of its role in environmental hazards, the nanotechnology-based smart devices, have attracted a great deal of attention, which have fulfilled a dual purpose of prevention and early warning. These tools can aid in the identification of health problems related to plants, prior to their detection by the farmers, as well as provide remedial steps. Additionally, they can also monitor chemical delivery. The user and eco-friendly delivery tools for pesticides and nutrients have established a significant position in the market, with minimal risk of environmental damage (Soutter, 2021). Also, companies have instigated the use of nanoemulsions in pesticide products, available commercially. Syngenta, formulates a microencapsulated product, exhibiting quick release, available in the market as Karate® ZEON (Soutter, 2021). The delivery of pesticides, insecticides, fungicides, growth hormones and nutrients, by multiple nano-formulations is enlisted in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanoparticles in plant disease management and nanopesticide-mediated prevention of pathogenic infestation
ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticle carrier systems, in form of nanomaterials, cups, carbon tubes, and so on, can be used to aid in the delivery of optimum chemicals, like pheromones, blockers of polyamine synthesis, SAR-inducing chemicals and active, concentrated forms of pesticides to enable controlled release, primarily during the flooded conditions (Khan et al., 2014). Therefore, to evaluate the impact of NPs in management of plant-related disorders, two approaches need to be considered, mainly, direct impact of NPs on pathogens, and nano-pesticides, where the role of nanomaterials is investigated in formulation of pesticides. Besides, NPs have also been reported to exhibit effective results in detection and diagnosis of plant diseases as well as analysis of pesticide residue, owing to the extremely small size and elevated degree of reactivity and sensitivity of NPs (Khan and Rizvi, 2014). The activity of phytopathogenic microbes is greatly affected by NPs due to their extremely small size (even smaller than viral particles) as well as great degree of reactivity. Certain metals, like silver (Ag NPs) has been found to hinder the colonization of Pseudomonas aeruginosa, S. aureus, Klebsiella pneumonia and Escherichia coli, where these NPs, developed from the leaf extracts of Ocimum tenuiflorum and solanum tricobactum, exhibited highest antimicrobial potential against E. coli and S. aureus (Khan and Rizvi, 2014). Jayaseelan et al. (2012) investigated the anti-bacterial effect of zinc NPs, where the bacteria targeted was P. aeruginosa, and the zone of inhibition was found to be maximum (22 ± 1.8 mm) at 25 ng m/L concentration of zinc oxide nanoparticles, thereby portraying these as NPs exhibiting optimum results for prevention of plant diseases. ZnO NPs have also been reported to exhibit antimicrobial actions against P. aeruginosa in a study (Aysa and Salman, 2016). Similarly, a hybrid nano-formulation, comprising of polyvinylpyrrolidone and Ag NPs, was developed and its anti-bacterial potential was assessed on three distinct bacterial groups, namely, E. coli, S. aureus and P. aeruginosa, along with spores of Bacillus subtilis (Bryaskova et al., 2011). Another study depicted the antimicrobial potential of AgNP-stabilized polyvinyl pyrrolidone formulation (Bhatia et al., 2021). Copper oxide NPs (CuO NPs) have been reported to exhibit anti-bacterial actions against P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, Bacillus subtilis and E. coli (Azam et al., 2012; Katwal et al., 2015). Potential bactericidal actions and antimicrobial ability has been recognized against gram positive bacteria, like P. aeruginosa, S. aureus and E. coli, by using Ag NPs. Physiology, concentration, metabolism and intracellular selective permeability of membranes of nanoparticles, along with type of microbe, influence the antibacterial ability of these particles (Khan and Rizvi, 2014).

Crop production is minimized due to actions of plant pathogens, such as fungi, bacteria, nematodes and viruses (Khan, 2012). Despite the use of multiple methods in managing the growth of pathogens, a complete control of phytopathogens could not be obtained (Khan and Jairajpuri, 2012), which elevated the need for nanotechnology to limit the extent of plant related disorders. Thus, nanotechnology has been reported to suppress the actions of disease-causing fungi, where a study has revealed the preventive role of Na2B4O7 and CuSo4 against rust disease of field peas (Singh et al., 2013). The charcoal rot disorders in sunflower have also been curbed by the use of zinc, manganese and other microelements (Abd El-Hai et al., 2009). The combination of Ag NPs with polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) was investigated for its fungicidal potential against multiple molds and yeasts, like Candida albicans, C. tropicalis, Aspergillus brasiliensis, C. Krusei etc., where the hybris was revealed to exhibit optimum antifungal effects (Bryaskova et al., 2011). Zinc oxide NPs (ZnO NPs) has also been found to exert fungicidal action against Penicillium expansum and Bortrytis cinerea, which were assessed by Raman spectroscopy, traditional microbiological plating and scanning electron microscopy (SEM), along with characterization of alterations in morphology and composition of cell of hyphae in the fungi (Khan and Rizvi, 2014). The growth of and P. expansum and B. Cinerea was greatly hindered by the ZnO NPs (>3 mmol/L), where the hyphae in B. cinerea were deformed and formation of conidia and conidiophores was ameliorated in P. expansum, resulting in destruction of the fungal hyphae (Khan and Rizvi, 2014). Furthermore, the colonization of Aspergillus flavus was found to be hindered by Zn NPs at a concentration of 25 mg/mL (Jayaseelan et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanopesticides: a dab hand at eliminating pests
ce:section.ce:para: The aid provided by nanotechnology in plant protection has resulted in exponential elevation in production of crop, by the use of nanopesticides. In the conventional crop protection, about 90% of the total pesticides applied, are either unable to reach the target location or lost in the environment (Nuruzzaman et al., 2016), which elevates the cost of production and alleviates the environmental systems. In order to ensure better plant protection from intervention of insects and pests, resulting in loss of crop. It is essential to consider the availability of active ingredients up to minimum effective concentration (MEC). The plant protection paradigm has been revolutionized by the advent nanoformulation or the use NP-encapsulated pesticides, which comprises of minute number of particles, containing active ingredients of pesticides (Haq and Ijaz, 2019). These ingredients are coated with another material (nanomaterial), where the former is referred to as the internal phase or the core material and the latter is called the external phase or the coating material (Nuruzzaman et al., 2016). Inside the plants in the root areas, the nanoformulations of NP-encapsulated pesticides enable the controlled release of the active ingredients, without depleting the efficacy, unlike the conventional pesticides which, besides minimizing the water solubility of pesticides, also induced to the harm to the organisms, resulting in increased resistance to the target beings (Dwivedi et al., 2016). The yield of the crop was reported to be boosted by nanopesticides, via elevation in the efficacy of pesticides, by pesticide transport regulation, where the combinations of nanoformulations with polymeric nanocapsules, and pyrethroid and bifenthrin, exhibited elevated elution with time and accelerated transport potential, even up on adding fertilizer loamy sand soil, saturated with cation containing artificial pore water (Petosa et al., 2017). Thus, this implies that pyrethroid could be used as an effective delivery vehicle and protective agent of pesticides. The organic solvent run off and undesired movement of the pesticides is reduced by the nanoformulations, on account of their increased dispersion ability and wettability. Furthermore, in the nanoformulation of pesticides, the NPs exhibited some useful characteristics like, enhanced permeability, thermal stability, crystallinity, solubility, stiffness as well as biodegradability, which is significant for the agro-environmental system (Haq and Ijaz, 2019). Also, the total amount of pesticides, which was required to control the spread of the disease and pest, was retarded by controlled and timely release of active ingredients, which turned out to be an important aspect of integrated pest management (IPM). This fulfils the demand of minimal agrochemical use, in order to facilitate environmental protection and mitigate damage to non-target organisms, simultaneously reducing the cost involved (Shang et al., 2019). It has been reported that due to diseases developed in plants, the agricultural crop losses, on a global scale, accounts for 2000 billion US$, that too only in the USA (Gonzalez-Fernandez et al., 2010; Rai and Ingle, 2012). Therefore, nanoformulations of pesticides, in this scenario, functions as a potential tool, to not only reduce the cost and amount of pesticides applied, but also enhance the growth and yield of the crop (Servin and Elmer, 2015). For example, clay nanotubes, halloysites, are economical pesticide carriers in the agricultural practice, where they exhibit extended release of active ingredients, as well as ensure limited environmental adversities (Dwivedi et al., 2016). A hydrophobic nano-silica facilitates insect mortality by getting absorbed into the cuticle layer of the organism upon contact (Nair et al., 2010). Further, the system acquired resistance of plants against the pests was found to be elevated by nanopesticides. For instance, the capability of pesticides, to permeate the plant and reach the cell sap, is elevated by the silica nanospheres, thus exhibiting a systemic effect in management of sucking or chewing insects, such as aphid (Li et al., 2007a,b). The damage to the plant via photodegradation is also prevented by these types of hollow nanoformulations (Panpatte et al., 2016). The non-systemic behavior of the pesticides is also reported to be changed by the use of nanoformulations (Hou et al., 2016). The formulation of tea leaves with metallic NPs results in the elevation of penetration potential, and alteration of the non-systemic behavior of ferbam. Therefore, these results would provide a new area for development of pesticide formulations, to enable plant-based systemic resistance. However, future investigations are required to comprehensively study the behavior of pesticides, and their interactive ability with macromolecules occurring in the biotic beans, as well as the environment. The catalytic ability of an important insect protease, trypsin was found to be retarded by bioactive Au NPs, fabricated with a latex, resulting in biological control of spread of damaging insects (Shang et al., 2019). This blockage might be because of the interaction between the proteins and metallic NPs, via covalent bonds, ionic interaction or by binding to the SH group of amino acid (Patil et al., 2016). Potential growth of Penicillium expansum, F. oxysporum, F. graminearum, P. aeruginosa, A. flavus, A. alternate and mucor plumbeus, is reported as a result of administration ZnO NPs (Servin and Elmer, 2015; Vanathi et al., 2016; Dwivedi et al., 2016). Unlike non-nano copper formulations in tomato, the copper NPs were found to exhibit effective results against Phytophthora infestans (Giannousi et al., 2013). The conidiophores and conidia in fungi are hindered by MONPs, resulting in death pf the fungal hyphae. Reduction in length of roots and shoots, dry and fresh weights, germination of seeds and photosynthesis was observed, along with reduction in weeds, on exposure to SiO2 NPs (Sharifi-Rad et al., 2016). A herbicide, pectin NPs, loaded with Metsulfuron methyl, exhibit greater cytotoxicity than commercial herbicide towards Chenopodium album plants.

The growth rate and yield of the seed is elevated by 33 and 20%, by the use of hydroxyapatite NPs, unlike the regular phosphorous fertilizers (Liu and Lal, 2015). Furthermore, the soil fertility can be maintained by slow and steady release of nanofertilizers, by reduction in transport of nutrients, into groundwater or run off, thereby eliminating the risk of environmental contamination and toxicity due to overuse (Liu and Lal, 2015). The foliar MWNTs are used as supplements in soil, which elevate the number of fruits and flowers in tomato plant, up to twice the original number, via gene/protein activation, which is significant for the growth and development of the plant (Khodakovskaya et al., 2013). However, despite all the investigations, featuring the use of nanofertilizers, their employment in agriculture is still limited. Even though, the use of metallic NPs has exhibited potential results in agriculture, yet their application is minimized dur to their disastrous effects on the non-target microbial communities existing in the soil, as a result of bioaccumulation (Goncalves et al., 2017; Shen et al., 2015; Simonin et al., 2016). In order to potentiate the controlled release of potassium, phosphorous and nitrogen in wheat, chitosan NPs are applied by foliar uptake (Abdel-Aziz et al., 2016). In the current scenario, despite the significance, more evidences are required, related to the advantages of nanofertilizers, over traditional and conventionally used fertilizers (Liu and Lal, 2015). Thus, stability of elevated along with solubility and a slow and effective targeted delivery is enabled, in case of NM-mediated delivery of pesticides (Duhan et al., 2017). The nanocapsular and nanospherical formulations of polymeric inorganic NMs can be employed as carrier systems for distribution of herbicides (Tanaka et al., 2012). Poly(Ɛ-caprolactone) is a polymeric NP, which exhibits appreciable characteristics of biological compatibility, and has been repetitively employed for atrazine (herbicide) encapsulation (Tanaka et al., 2012). Similarly, chitosan is another polymer, whose nanoformulation, incorporated with 3 triazine herbicides, has been reported to decrease the environmental toxicity and lower the gene toxicity impact in Allium cepa (Grillo et al., 2015).

Furthermore, nanoemulsions function as suitable delivery systems for pesticides, on account of better kinetic stability, limited viscosity, optical transparency and small sizes (Xu et al., 2019), which in turn improve the bioavailability and solubility of active ingredients in the pesticide formulation. Some important delivery techniques of nanopesticides comprise of nanocontainers, nanoencapsulates, nanocages and nanoemulsions, which have been proven to be significant in plant protection programs (Bergeson et al., 2010b). The release of NPK fertilizer is enabled by the use of chitosan NPs, which is a highly degradable, anti-microbial polymer (Corradini et al., 2010). Nanolayers, based up on kaolin clay, have also been developed for their use as coating materials to enable controlled release of fertilizers (Sempeho et al., 2015). The polyhydroxyl alkanates (microbial polymer) have proved their efficacy in the controlled release of pesticides (Khan and Rizvi, 2014). Intelimer, i.e., a temperature-sensitive polymer regulates pesticide release seasonally, based upon fluctuations in the temperature, thus providing protection to the active ingredients, from degradation and leaching (Khan and Rizvi, 2014). Coated granules, microcapsules, granular matrices and microspheres are some of the significant controlled release devices, that can be employed in nanopesticide formulations.

The modification of NMs using sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS) has elevated the DMM absorption. The nanoformulations of Ag/TiO2 imidacloprid was modified by SDS, followed by microencapsulation by using alginate and chitosan, which was then tested on soybean, where the results indicated rapid degradation of these residues in plants and soil, during the initial 8 days, and were almost undetectable, after 20 days had passed. The SDS was employed to elevate the photo-degradation of NPs in the soil. The nanosensors based on NMs have also been employed to detect the residues of pesticides, as a substitute for gas/liquid chromatography-mass spectroscopy hyphenated methods. There are about 1050 chemicals, as per the FDA, which have been recognized as pesticide residues (FDA, 2005). Besides, being reliable and accurate, the traditional techniques occupy a lot of time.

The diseases caused due to fungi formulate a primary factor, that results in loss of production. Even though, a number of commercial fungicides are available in the market, yet their harmful effects on plant are also quite known. This issue can be solved by using nanotechnology-based fungicides, where NPs are used as anti-fungal agents are used against disease-causing fungi (Duhan et al., 2017). NPs of Ag, TiO2 and ZnO have been evaluated for their anti-fungal activity against macrophomina phaseolina, in oil seed and pulse. In case of Ag NPs, greater anti-fungal activity was reported at lower concentrations as compared to TiO2 and ZnO NPs (Duhan et al., 2017). NPs have been investigated for their efficacy against Aspergillus niger and Fusarium oxysporum, in comparison with bulk silica in maize, where the plant when treated with nano-Si, exhibited greater number of phenolic compounds, as observed in leaf extracts and lower amounts of stress-responsive enzymes, against these fungi (Duhan et al., 2017). Therefore, greater resistance in case of nano-silica – treated maize, was observed, as compared to bilk silica, with respect to disease index and expression of phenyl alanine ammonia lyase, phenols, polyphenol oxidase and peroxidase., thus, establishing the potential of Si NPs, as anti-fungal agents (Suriyaprabha et al., 2014). On account of ability of silver to actovate the thiol groups in the cell wall of fungi, which causes disruption of transmembrane, ETC and energy metabolic pathway. Silver has been recognized as a potential and superios anti-fungal agents, as compared to other metals (Duhan et al., 2017). DNA mutation in fungi, enzyme complex (necessary for respiratory chain) impairment, retarded permeability of the membrane and breakdown of the cell are some of the other mechanisms involved (Velmurugan et al., 2009). The shape and size of the particle of silver NPs are some factors. The truncated triangular shape of the particles, exhibits greater cidal action, as compared to rod and spherical shaped NPs (Pal et al., 2007; Panáček et al., 2006). In plant disease management, the anti-fungal potential of silver NPs has been extensively utilized (Karimi et al., 2012). Sustained and well-diffused solution of Ag NPs, functions as an appreciable fungicide, on account of good adhesive properties on fungal and bacterial cell wall (Kim et al., 2009).

Similar to fungi, weeds are other organisms that pose a significant threat to the agricultural production. Irrespective of the conventional herbicides, the time-consuming nature of these fertilizers exhibit a dire need to develop nanotechnology-based herbicides. The conventional herbicides exert damaging effects on the plants, as well as retard the fertility of the soil, elevating soil contamination (Duhan et al., 2017). This can be eliminated by providing an environmental-friendly approach by using nanoherbicides, which do not leave any detrimental residues in the environment and soil ecosystem (Pérez-de-Luque an Rubiales, 2009). Polymer-mediated herbicide encapsulation also promotes safety of the environment (Kumar et al., 2015). Non-judicial use of herbicides, that too long period of time, leaved harmful residues in soil, exhibiting detrimental effects on the crops (Chinnamuthu and Boopathi, 2009). Also, resistance against the herbicide develops, due to constant use of the same herbicide for a long period of time. The nano-zero valent iron have been evaluated for its herbicidal action against atrazine (Satapanajaru et al., 2008). The herbicide-loaded specific NPs have been developed for targeted delivery of chemicals into the roots of weeds, which enter via translocation and blockage of metabolic pathways, resulting in death of the plant (Nair et al., 2010; Ali, 2014). Atrazine and ametryn, containing poly(Ɛ-caprolactone) nanocapsules have been reported to exhibit toxic effects against algae, Pseudokirchneriella subcapitata, as well as microcrustacean, Daphnia similis (Duhan et al., 2017). These nanocapsules, when encapsulate herbicides, lower the toxicity towards the algae and elevate the toxicity towards the microcrustacean, unlike individual application of herbicides (Clemente et al., 2014). Table 2 enlists the nanomaterials, and the respective phytopathogen they act on.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials greasing the wheels of plant growth and development
ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology is a novel approach which aids in improvement of plant-associated functions, such as germination of seeds, growth and development of plants, as well as enrichment of crop quality. As a sensitive stage in the plant life cycle, seed germination facilitates development of the seedling, population dynamics and survival of the plant. Still, this process is extensively influenced by environmental and genetic factors, availability and non-availability of moisture as well as fertility of soil. Multiple studies have indicated the positive impact of NMs in germination of seeds and plant growth and development. For instance, multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) exert appositive impact on the germination process of crops, such as corn, tomato, barley, maize, soybean, garlic and peanut (Khodakovskaya and Biris, 2010; Joshi et al., 2018). The germination of seeds has been found to be improved by application of SiO2 NPs, zeolite and TiO2 NPs (Lu et al., 2002). In maize and barley, iron and silicon dioxide NPs have been reported to improve the seed germination process. However, even though many studies have focused on the potential role of NPs in seed germination, this area still requires further research. The seed coat penetrating ability of NPs have been established in certain investigations, where such particles have been observed to elevate water absorption, this upregulating the enzymatic system, resulting in improved germination and growth of the seedling (Lu et al., 2002; Banerjee and Kole, 2016). However, the data related to the water uptake, induced by NPs, still need further exploration. Besides germination, plant growth and development, along with crop quality has also been improved by NP formulations, like TiO2, MWCNTs, FeO, ZnO, hydroxyfullerenes and Zn–Fe–Cu-oxide NPs (Dubey et al., 2016; Shojaei et al., 2019a,b). The hydroxyl functionalized fullerenes, carbon NPs fulerols, were reported to exhibit a positive impact on the growth of the plants. The hypocotyl growth was observed to be increased in Arabidopsis, by increased cell division, as a result of fullerenes (Gao et al., 2011). Besides, the enhancement in the number, yield and size of the fruit up to 128%, the amount of bioactive agents, like lycopene, cucurbitacin-B, charantin and inulin in bitter melon, was also found to be increased, by administration of fullerol (Kole et al., 2013). A study depicted the role of nano-iron fertilizer in enhancing the agronomic characteristics of Dracocephalum moldavica and improvement in the content of essential oils in the plant (Yousefzadeh and Sabaghnia, 2016). The yield and quality of fruit was enhanced by the foliar application of zinc and boron NP fertilizers, with about 4.4–7.6% elevation in total soluble solids (TSS), resulting in titratable acidity by 9.5–29.1%, enhanced maturity index by 20.6–46.1% and elevated pH of pomegranate juice by 0.28–0.62 units, without exerting any impact on the physical characteristics of the fruit (Davarpanah et al., 2016). The ability of NMs, to increase nutrient and water absorption, enhancing the strength of the roots and elevating the activity of the enzymes, partly gives a mechanistic explanation behind its contribution in increasing the growth and quality of the crop (Dubey et al., 2016; Shojaei et al., 2019a,b). Furthermore, urea fertilizer, coated with hydroxyapatite NM, slowly promotes nitrogen release, for up to 60 days, unlike the conventional fertilizer, which carrier out imbalanced release, that too for just 30 days, thus reducing the plant nutrient efficiency and negatively affecting the growth of the crop (Kottegoda et al., 2011). However, certain studies also depict negative effects of NPs on the plant growth and development, seed germination and quality of crop (Cox et al., 2017). At a concentration of 2.5%, TiO2 was found to elevate the photosynthetic process in spinach, which was reduced when the NM concentration surpassed 4% (Zheng et al., 2005). The iron and SiO2 NPs increased the length of the shoot, when applied at a concentration of 15 mg/kg, however, the opposite happened, when the concentration was increased to 25 mg/kg (Disfani et al., 2017). Thus, this suggests the growth of the plant is dependent upon the concentration of NM applied. Besides, the mode of application of NM also seemed to regulate the growth of the plant, where foliar application of Fe3O4 NPs elevated the total content of carbohydrates, chlorophyll, essential oils and iron, along with height of the plant, branches, leaves, fresh and dry weight of Ocimum basilicum, unlike soil application (Shang et al., 2019). Multiple physiological and morphological alterations are observed as a result of interaction between NPs and plants, where the potential of these particles id determined by the chemical composition, reactivity, surface covering and effective dose (Khodakovskaya et al., 2012). The nano-SiO2, applied exogenously, on seedlings of Larix olgensis, resulted in improved growth seedling, along with enriched quality of the plant, elevated mean height of the plant and root, increased diameter and number of roots, along with induction of chlorophyll synthesis. Moreover, SiO2 NMs also stimulate seed germination under conditions of abiotic stress. Under salinity stress, the antioxidant system and germination of seeds is elevated by SiO2 NMs in squash and tomato plants (Haghighi et al., 2012; Siddiqui and Al-Whaibi, 2014a). The MPs of palladium, gold, sopper and silica exert important impact on the lettuce seeds (Shah and Belozerova, 2009). The germination of seeds in soybean is improved by administration of TiO2 and SiO2 NPs, by increasing nitrate reductase enzyme and increasing the water and nutrient absorbing ability of the seeds (Lu et al., 2002; Zheng et al., 2005). The dry and fresh weight of leaves is improved, along with content of chlorophyll as well as accumulation of proline, by SiO2 NPs, under salinity stress. As a result of aggregation of free amino acids, proline, nutrients and increased activity of antioxidant enzymes, by SiO2 NPs, the tolerance of plants to abiotic stress in improved (Kalteh et al., 2014; Siddiqui and Al-Whaibi, 2014b; Haghighi et al., 2012; Li et al., 2012). A quantum dot-treated rice plant was investigated, and it was reported that the silica-coated quantum dot treatment, induced more root growth, as compared to quantum dots alone (Wang et al., 2014). The growth and development of plants is enhanced by nano-SiO2, via enhancement in gas exchange, parameters of chlorophyll fluorescence, like rate of transpiration, activity of PS2, acute and effective photochemical efficacy, net rate of photosynthesis, rate of transport of electrons and conductance of the stomata (Siddiqui and Al-Whaibi, 2014c). Increased growth and development of plants by administration of zinc oxide (ZnO) NPs, has also been shown in multiple studies. ZnO NPs, in lower concentration levels, has indicated beneficial impact on germination of seeds in peanut (Prasad, 2012), soybean (Sedghi et al., 2013), wheat (Ramesh et al., 2014) and onion (Raskar and Laware, 2014). However, elevated levels of these NPs have been reported to disrupt the process of germination of seeds. Varying concentrations of ZnO NPs were applied on tomato, alfalfa grass and cucumber, and it was observed that germination of seeds only in cucumber was increased. The improved biomass, growth of root and shoot, area of root, production of protein and chlorophyll, synthesis of rhizopheric microbes, alkaline and acid phosphatase, as well as elevated activity of phytase enzyme, was observed in cluster bean rhizosphere in Cyamopsis tetragonoloba plant, when administered with ZnO NPs. The seedling roots of Cicer arietinum and Vigna radiata were revealed to improve the length and biomass of shoot and root, after ZnO NP absorption, as confirmed by coupled plasma and atomic emission spectroscopy and light and scanning microscope (Mahajan et al., 2011). After supplementation with MS media, the ZnO NPs induced plantlet regeneration, shooting, somatic embryogenesis, stimulate production of proline, enhanced activity of superoxide dismutase, peroxidase and catalase enzyme, this improving the tolerance of plants towards environmental stress (Helaly et al., 2014).

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are considered to be the most significant NMs, on account of their electrical, chemical, thermal and mechanical characteristics, due to which they acquire the ability to permeate the cell membrane and cell wall, alongside providing a reliable delivery system for chemical transportation to cells. The single-walled CNTs (SWCNTs) are recognized as efficient delivery systems (nanotransporters) for movement of dye molecules and DNA across the cells of the plants (Srinivasan and Saraswathi, 2010). The efficiency of nutrient and water uptake is also upregulated by MWCNTs, resulting in elevated germination of seeds as well as growth and development of plants (Tiwari et al., 2014). On account of the ability of MWCNTs to permeate the seed coats, they induce accelerated seed germination in corn, barley and soybean, when added to the sterile agar medium, as according to the Transmission electron spectroscopy (TES) and Raman Spectroscopy, the nanotube aggregates were identified within the seed coats (Lahiani et al., 2013). Furthermore, in the similar plants as above, MWCNTs were reported to regulate the gene expression encoding, in multiple types of water channel proteins. MWCNTs induced maximum rate of germination in hybrid BT cotton, rice, tomato, Phaseolus mungo and Brassica juncea (Nalwade and Neharkar, 2013). Besides positively regulating germination of seeds and growth of plants, MWCNTs, were also observed to stimulate the growth of cell culture of tobacco, by elevating the marker genes for division of cells by CycB, formation of cell wall by NtLRX1 and movement of water molecules by aquaporin (Khodakovskaya et al., 2012). The cell elongation was elevated in the root system and activity of dehydrogenase was enhanced by oxidized MWCNTs (Wang et al., 2012a). still, certain studies have indicated a negative impact of MWCNTs on germination of seeds, despite elevating the concentration of NMs to high levels (Husen and Siddiqi, 2014). The improvement in the growth of shoot and roots, as well as activity of dehydrogenase and peroxidase enzyme, due to MWCNTs, may be because of MWCNT uptake and accumulation, that precedes the translocation from roots to the leaves, resulting in induction of gene expression (Khodakovskaya et al., 2012; Lahiani et al., 2013).
