CNTs, which were water soluble, were found inside the wheat plants, via fluorescence and scanning electron microscopy, where they were revealed to promote the growth of shoor and root in dark as well as light conditions (Tripathi and Sarkar, 2014). The biomass and water retention ability has also been improved by MWCNTs, along with elevation in the plant medicinal properties (Khodakovskaya et al., 2013; Husen and Siddiqi, 2014). Certain studies have indicated the toxic impact of gold NPs on plants, as they are considered to hinder the functions of transportation protein, aquaporin, in the plant body (Shah and Belozerova, 2009). Still, these NPs have been reported to augment germination of seeds, in plants like Cucumis sativus, Brassica juncea, Lactuca sativa, Gloriosa superba and Boswellia ovaligoliolata. The area and number of leaves, height of the plant, content of sugar and chlorophyll, is improved as a result of Au NPs administration, resulting in better production of crop (Gopinath et al., 2014). The genome of soybean was introduced with neomycin phosphotransferase 2 gene, via Au NPs, coated with DNA (Christou et al., 1988). The antioxidant system and seed germination in Arabidopsis thaliana was augmented as a result of Au NPs, along with changes in the concentration of microRNA expression, which mediates numerous physiological, metabolic and morphological plant processes.

The impact of silver NPs was investigated on the growth metabolism of hydroponically grown Bacopa monnieri, where the results depicted a potential impact on the germination of seeds, induction of carbohydrate and protein synthesis, reduction in total phenolic content and activities of peroxidase and catalase enzymes (Krishnaraj et al., 2012). The seedling growth and germination of seeds was also found to be elevated by Ag NPs in Boswellia ovaliofoliolata trees (Savithramma et al., 2012). The growth parameters of plants, like area of the leaves and length of the shoot and root, along with biochemical aspects, like contents of protein and carbohydrate, chlorophyll and antioxidant enzymes, of corn, common bean and Brassica juncea are also enhanced by Ag NPs (Salama, 2012; Sharma, 2012). Still, both positive and negative effects of these NPs have been observed, on elongation of the roots, which depends upon the species of the plant (Gruyer and Dorais, 2013) For instance, barley exhibited elevated root length, whereas in lettuce, root elongation was hindered. Out of the 11 wetland species of plants, a study reported that Ag NPs caused increased rate of germination of seeds, only in one of them, namely, Eupatorium fistulosum (Yin et al., 2012). The ethylene signaling is hindered by Ag NPs in Crocus sativus, as a result of which growth of root takes place (Rezvani et al., 2012). The size and conformation of the NPs regulate the effect of Ag NPs on the physiological and morphological aspects of plants. Out of three varying Ag NPs morphologies, an investigation revealed that decahedral conformation of Ag NPs depicted maximum root growth promotion (RGP), which is not at all altered by the spherical shaped Ag NPs (Syu et al., 2014). The spherical morphology, however, induced maximum level of accumulation of anthocyanin in seedlings of Arabidopsis (Syu et al., 2014). The decahedral conformation exhibits the lowest, while the spherical conformation exhibits the highest values for copper and zinc superoxide dismutase. Furthermore, Ag NPs stimulated blockage of root elongation, via activation of aminocyclopropane 1-carboxylic acid (ACC), in Arabidopsis seedlings, along with retarded expression of ACC oxidase 2 and ACC synthase 7, revealing the role of Ag NPs in blocking the ethylene perception, as well as in interfering with the biological synthesis of ethylene.

Similarly, multiple studies have evaluated the role of TiO2 NPs on multiple organisms, however, its definitive role in plants is yet to be explored. The growth of the radicle and the pumule was promoted, along with elevated germination of seeds, by TiO2 NPs, in canola seedlings (Mahmoodzadeh et al., 2013). Also, the growth of wheat was improved, parallel to increased yield, even under water defiant conditions (Jaberzadeh et al., 2013). The activity of enzymes involved in metabolism of nitrogen, is regulated by these NPs, which leads to nitrate absorption by plants and promotes transformation of inorganic to organic nitrogen in the form of chlorophyll and protein, which elevates the dry and fresh weight of the plant (Mishra et al., 2014). TiO2 NPs functions as a light-based catalyst (photocatalyst) and promotes induction of a redox reaction. Also, these NPs elevate the strength of the aged seeds and induced formation of chlorophyll, along with stimulation of activity of Rubisco (ribulose 1,5-biphosphate carboxylase) and enhancement of photosynthesis, resulting in plant growth and development (Yang et al., 2006). The light absorbance is also increased by TiO2 NPs, which in turn accelerates the transportation and transformation of light energy, resulting in protection of chloroplasts from aging and prolongation of photosynthesis (Yang et al., 2006). Table 3 provides a comparative data related to the positive and negative impact of NMs on plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanofertilizers: a sustainable alternative to conventional means
ce:section.ce:para: Fertilizer supplementation is essential for improving the productivity of the crop and fertility of the soil (Li et al., 2018), which renders these chemicals a significant position in the development of sustainable agriculture (Davarpanah et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2017). Global food issues have reached to the peak in the current scenario, due to limited natural resources and constant progression in human population, which would require 60–100% more food to fulfil the needs of the growing population (Dijk and Meijerink, 2014). The fertility of the soil and yield of the agricultural field is minimized by intensive farming, which is practiced in order to meet the desired food needs. About 40% of the agricultural fields, across the globe, have been damaged due to loss of fertility of soil, as a result of uncontrollable farming practices (Kale and Gawade, 2016). Therefore, fertilizers are applied in large quantities to conserve the soil fertility and productivity of the crop (Li et al., 2014). It is considered that the use of fertilizers regulates the productivity of one-third of the crops, while other agricultural factors control the remaining production. However, the effective use of fertilizers does not surpass 30–40% (Dijk and Meijerink, 2014). In addition to this, in case of conventional fertilizers, the nutrient use efficiency is dependent on the overall concentration of fertilizers, which reach the target site (Solanki et al., 2015). Actually, the amount < than the minimum desired concentration, is able to reach the site of target, because of loss of chemicals due to leaching, runoff, hydrolytic and photolytic reactions, drift, degradation by microbes and evaporation (Sabir et al., 2014). The inherent nutrient equilibrium of the soil particles is disturbed as a result of repetitive application of fertilizers in excessive amounts. Additionally, these chemicals pose a serious danger to the environment as well due to leaching of toxic compounds into water bodies and soil, resulting in water and land contamination (Solanki et al., 2015). The scenario of global agriculture has been enhanced by the revolution in green nanotechnology, NMs are used as nanofertilizers, exhibiting potential results to meet the global demand for food and sustainable agriculture (Shang et al., 2019). These agents serve as optimized tools to increase the nutrient use efficiency, in case of deficiency of micro- and macro-nutrients, as well as overcome the issues related to eutrophication (Shukla et al., 2019). The convention fertilizers, based upon nitrogen, exhibit the great losses, via leaching, evaporation and 50–70% degradation, resulting in retardation of efficiency of fertilizers and enhancement of production costs (Miao et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015). On the contrary, the nanoformulations of nitrogen-based fertilizers, i.e., nitrogen nanofertilizers, inhibited the nutrient losses, by direct internalization, this hindering the interactions of nutrients with water, microbes, soil and air (Dwivedi et al., 2016; Panpatte et al., 2016). For example, the nitrogen losses are significantly reduced by the administration of porous NMs, like zeolites, chitsan and clay, via regulation of release based upon demand and elevating the plant uptake process (Millan et al., 2008; Panpatte et al., 2016; Abdel-Aziz et al., 2016). The solubility of phosphate minerals id elevated by the ammonium charged zeolites, resulting in improved availability of phosphorous and uptake by plants (Dwivedi et al., 2016). The NMs based upon carbon, Graphene oxide films, can increase the release of potassium nitrate, which increases the time span of function and retards leaching losses (Shalaby et al., 2016). The significance of NMs has also been increased in the production of crops, unlike the conventional fertilizers (Sabir et al., 2014), where combined application of nanocalcite (CaCO3; 40%) and nano SiO2 (4%), along with MgO (1%) as well as Fe2O3 (1%), enhanced the calcium, iron and magnesium uptake, as well as increased phosphorous intake with micronutrients manganese and zinc (Shang et al., 2019). On the basis of the actions, nanofertilizers have been sub-divided into control loss fertilizers, control and slow release fertilizers, nanocomposite or magnetic fertilizers (Panpatte et al., 2016; Lateef et al., 2016).

Balanced and judicial management of fertilizers, use of seeds with superior quality and irrigation can increase the agricultural yield by 35–40%. For instance, the grain production of soybean, rice, winter wheat, vegetables and spring maize, was found to be elevated by 16.74%, 10.29%, 28.81%, 12.34–19.76% and 10.93%, with the use of carbon-based nanofertilizer (Liu et al., 2009). The chitosan-NPK fertilizer elevate the mobilization, crop and harvest index on wheat variables, unlike the control yield variables (Abdel-Aziz et al., 2016). The plant nutrient uptake, surface area of leaves and plant root elongation are the significant facets, which are improved by the application of nanofertilizers (Shang et al., 2019). The nutrient uptake of plants is increased by forming complexes with root exudates and molecular transporters, via production of novel pores, or by exposing ion channels or endocytosis (Mastronardi et al., 2015). The surface mass ratio of NPs is increased, as a result of reduction in size of NMs, which increases nutrient absorption and desorption at a slow and steady rate, for a prolonged period of time (Monreal et al., 2016; Subramanian et al., 2015). Besides the macronutrients, the micronutrients, such as boron, copper, manganese, zinc, molybdenum, chlorine and so on, also play an essential role in facilitating sustainable agriculture. Still. Multiple soil-related factors, like pH of the soil particles promote the deficiency of these particles in the production of crop, with extensive farming practice (Ghormade et al., 2011). Not only the crop productivity, but also the human health is retarded by micronutrient deficiency. For example, deficiency of iron leads to impairment of growth, anemia, cognitive decline, retarded physical strength and issues related to reproductive health (Monreal et al., 2016; Swaminathan et al., 2013). The nanoformulation of micronutrients for controlled or slow nutrient release, would promote plant uptake, increase growth and productivity of plants, aiding in maintenance of soil health (Peteu et al., 2010). For example, administration of ZnO (at minute doses) on soil, defiant in zinc, stimulated the physiological profile and growth of the plant, like fresh and dry weight, length of root and shoor, as well as photosynthetic processes, unlike the control (Ali et al., 2019; Asl et al., 2019). The productivity of barley was increased by 91% by application of nanofertilizers, based up on ZnO NPs, unlike only 31% elevation in the productivity by traditional ZnSO4 nanofertilizers (Kale and Gawade, 2016). The practice of maintaining a sustainable agriculture has introduced the terminology, “control loss fertilizer” which has been developed with an aim to mitigate the non-point pollution of agricultural inputs, which form a nano-network by assembling with its own particles, up on coming in contact with water in soil (Jiang et al., 2006). The entrapped nutrient of the fertilizer, on the contrary, penetrate the soil by surface tension, hydrogen bonding and molecular forces, resulting in the enlargement of their spatial scales, facilitating easy inhibition by soil filtration, remaining fixed in soil via crop roots, which permits absorption of nutrients by plants to fulfil the requirement of the requirement of the plant growth cycle (Shang et al., 2019). Besides reducing loss due to leaching by 24.5% and nitrogen runoff by 21.6%, the control loss fertilizer is also reported to increase the residual mineral nitrogen of the soil by 9.8%, with 5.5% elevation in production of wheat, unlike conventional fertilizers (Shang et al., 2019). However, this novel concept demands more research and investigation, to accurately explore the future significance of such substances (Shang et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanotechnology-mediated accelerated plant adaptation to climatic alterations
ce:section.ce:para: Alteration in the climate baseline, over considerable amount of time induces progressive climate change, such as deficiency of water, temperature changes, alkalinity, cold, salinity and toxic metal-mediated pollution of the environment. Thus, the primary concern is to enhance the plant adaptation, without compromising the sensitive ecosystems in existence, as they already strive to adapt to the stresses, in the environment (Vermeulen et al., 2012). Significant strategies are required to accomplish such tasks, such as plant enzymatic system activation, regulation of plant hormones, and uptake of toxic metals, stress gene expression, and mitigating stress due to water deficiency or flash floods, via abbreviating the life cycle of the plant (Shang et al., 2019). Nanofertilizers can elevate crop production during environmental stresses, as suggested by accelerating progression in nanomaterial engineering. The crop production is hindered by salinity stress in approximately 23% of agricultural lands across the globe (Onaga and Kerstin, 2016). On the other hand, SiO2 NPs enhance the fresh and dry weight of plants, elevate content of chlorophyll, with accumulation of proline in squash and tomato plants, under saline stress, as well as enhanced germination of seeds (Siddiqui and Al-Whaibi, 2014a; Haghighi et al., 2012). Similarly, the foliar spray of FeSO4 NPs has been shown to exhibit an appreciable response towards tolerating the salinity stress in sunflower, where their application elevated the dry weight of the shoor, concentration of sub-optimal CO2, leaf area, net CO2 assimilation, content of chlorophyll, as well as photochemical potential of photosystem 2 and iron content (Torabian et al., 2017). However, it alleviated the concentration of sodium in the leaves. Silicon NPs have been reported to reduce the stress induced by UV-B radiation in wheat plant (Tripathi et al., 2017). The long-term availability of nutrients has been improved by nano-zeolite, which has also elevated plant growth and germination (Shang et al., 2019). Furthermore, the life cycle of wheat plant, incorporated with nanofertilizers, was found to be shorter by 23.5% for yield production, from the date of sowing, unlike the plants administered with conventional fertilizers. This elevation in productivity and growth due to nanofertilizers portray the potential of these chemicals in agricultural fields, primarily in the areas prone to droughts as well as flood prone regions, where early maturity of plants is a significant asset to sustainable production of crop (Shang et al., 2019). Additionally, NMs have been reported to exhibit effective results in detoxification of dangerous pollutants. Foliar administration of nano-silica (2.5 mm) has been revealed to enhance the tolerance of rice plants towards cadmium stress, by managing the accumulation of the metal (Shang et al., 2019). Besides cadmium, nanosilica was also found to be effective against metals, like zinc, copper and lead, which indicated its significance in ameliorating accumulation of heavy metals (Wang et al., 2016).

The biotic factors, like diseases and pests, greatly affect the productivity of the crop (Oerke, 2006). The farmers have been greatly dependent on pesticides to limit the losses in crop production, which however, has exhibited harmful and detrimental effects on the health of the living organisms as well as stability of the environment. An investigation has reported the applications of NPs in amelioration of attack by pests as well as prevalence of diseases, thus mitigating the extent of losses in yield and issues related to the environment (Shang et al., 2019). Ag NPs were biosynthesized with the help of stem extracts of Gossypium hirsutum (cotton), and has been reported to exhibit appreciable anti-bacterial actions, which were observed by calculating the zone of inhibition for Xanthomonas campestris, a disease-causing bacteria, for Brassicaceae and Malvaceae families of plants (Vanti et al., 2019). Many soil- and plant-associated diseases are observed to be managed by metal-based NPs, like MgO, CuO and ZnO, which have been shown to limit the disorders caused by Alternaria alternate, Fusarium solani, Verticillium dahlia, Fusarium oxysporum, Monilinia fructicola and so on (Giannousi et al., 2013; Imada et al., 2016)). Therefore, a proper use of NMs can elevate the crop productivity, alongside limiting the negative impact on the environment. The elevated efficiency and environment-friendly nature of nanocomposites have been exploited by the researchers in the recent years (Gomathi et al., 2019). Silver-chitsan nanocomposites, in combination with antracol (fungicide) have depicted enhanced anti-fungal actions, unlike either of the two components individually (Le et al., 2019). The nanocomposites of Bacillus thuringiensis, have been reported to increase the efficiency and shelf life of pesticides, as well as abbreviate the initial dosage required (Shang et al., 2019). However, actual mechanisms, justifying the role of nanocomposites based up on Bacillus thuringiensis are still further to be investigated (Devi et al., 2019). The accumulation of amino acids and free proline is enhanced by application of ZnO NPs and SiO2 NPs, along with increased uptake of water and nutrients and improved actions of antioxidant enzymes, resulting in improvement in tolerance of plants towards unfavorable climatic conditions (Wang et al., 2015). Additionally, the NMs were also found to control the expression of stress genes. For instance, the number of genes, either upregulated or downregulated were portrayed as a result of a microarray analysis, by administration of silver NPs in Arabidopsis (Banerjee and Kole, 2016). Majority of the gene are related to the response to oxidative stress and metals, among the upregulated genes, whereas the downregulated genes are associated with response to hormonal stimuli and pathogens (Banerjee and Kole, 2016). Such response induced by NMs are directly related to protection of plant against the stresses (Onaga and Kerstin, 2016). Still, the plant response to nanofertilizers is different, with variation in species, distinct stages of growth and different types of NMs used (Abdel-Aziz et al., 2016). Thus, future considerations should focus on identification of signaling cascades, as well as specific NM regulated genes in varying species of plants (Shang et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The bitter end of nanotechnology in agriculture
ce:section.ce:para:
Toxic impact of NPs has been revealed, in impeding the growth of the plant, elongation of shoot and root, biomass yield, photosynthetic processes and contents of chlorophyll, along with increased actions of catalase and peroxidase enzymes in the roots of treated plants (Acharya and Pal, 2020). The phytotoxicity profile of NPs, is associated with NM shape, surface or size mediated induction of stress or stimulus, as well as chemical composition-dependent chemical toxicity of NMs (Maghsoodi et al., 2019). The NPs can significantly interact with and enter the surrounding environment, on account of their minute size, where they then function as eco-toxicological hazards (Maghsoodi et al., 2019). The prime aim of this section is to evoke the limitations associated with the use of NPs in agriculture, with an objective to recognize and resolve the bitter end of this technology and promote safe and reliable use of NPs in farming practices. Fig. 3
portrays the destructive impact of NPs on growth, development and productivity of crops (see Fig. 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Harmful effects of nanoparticles on plants
ce:section.ce:para: Engineered NMs (ENMs) are intentionally-manufactured nano-scale materials (Yokel and MacPhail, 2011), which are cultivated from bulk materials to promote bizarre interactions with small-scale biomolecules. They exhibit unusual properties at cellular, atomic as well as molecular levels, depending upon their geometry, which influences their behavior and applications (Urbanska et al., 2019). Besides their positive applications, they also exhibit a negative impact on plants, during numerous stages of vegetation, such as germination of seeds, root elongation and growth, development of shoots and seedlings, ability to carry out photosynthesis, reproductive behavior and yield properties (Zhang et al., 2018b; Huang et al., 2017; Du et al., 2017; Deng et al., 2017). The rate of growth of roots of wheat plant have been reported to be curbed by copper up to 60% (approximately) and the development of lateral roots has been stimulated by the metal, which had induced oxidative stress (Zhang et al., 2018). TiO2 has been reported to aid in phytocatalysis in water treatment process, during soil remediation. Only the smallest amount of TiO2 (with size<36 nm), accumulated in the parenchyma of the wheat root, however, unable to reach to the shoot and stele, not inducing any harmful effects, on the germination rate, transpiration, ability to carry out photosynthesis and biomass of the wheat plant (Qiana et al., 2020). Despite this observation, TiO2 (25 nm) was observed to exhibit dangerous effects on legume plants after exposure for 60 days, thus blocking the growth of the plant by 17%, ameliorating the number of eaves by 29%, and reducing the biomass of the shoot by 53% (Vijayaraj et al., 2018). The other transcriptome analysis results depicted the genotoxic impact of ZnO on roots and shoots of the maize plant, however, it was observed that the gene response in both the areas of the plant vary, where the number of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) in root was found to be more (Xun et al., 2017). The negative impact of the engineered NMs is significantly related to the bioaccessibility, size and solubility of these particles, along with the physicochemical characteristics, time duration and test dost of the plant specimen considered (Josko et al., 2017). Properties of the soil time of contact, condition of light and characteristic of the organisms are other environmental factors, which have a significant impact on the nature of engineered NMs in the soil ecosystem and their resulting impact (Hou et al., 2019; Dimkpa, 2018; Huang et al., 2017). The toxic effects of ZnO were observed on Lepidium sativum, with respect to the growth of the root, which was closely related to the amount applied in the soil samples, as well as environmental factors, like light, time of contact and temperatures (Josko and Oleszczuk, 2013). Similarly, the toxicity profile and bioaccumulation of ZnO on Phaseolus vulgaris plant (bean) was reported due to availability of a bacterium Pseudomonas chlororaphis, which is associated with the root, due to release of siderophores by the organism, that ameliorated the metal uptake by the bean plant (Dimkpa et al., 2015a,b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanoparticles impeding human health and disrupting the soil ecosystem
ce:section.ce:para: Besides the terrestrial plants, the engineered NMs also put the organisms found in the soil in grave danger. Numerous types of NMs, such as carbon-based NPs, like graphene oxides, C70 and CNTs; metal oxide NPs, like CuO, TiO2 and ZnO; and nZVI are under extensive investigation for their impact on the soil organisms (Hou et al., 2017; Schlich et al., 2016). ZnO was considered to be the most toxic, on account of its ability to induce 100% mortality of E. coli, B. subtilis and P. fluorescens, while CuO was referred to exhibit greater toxicity than ZnO, with respect to P. chlororaphis 06, which is a beneficial bacterium (Jiang et al., 2009). Genetoxic effects on the microbial cell, reproduction related toxicity, developmental harm and blockage of the activity of soil enzymes, as well as altered structure of microbial community are significant hazards of ENMs, to the microorganisms found in the soil (Asadishad et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2017). The negative effects of C20 as well as reduced graphene oxide were investigated on the composition of soil bacterial community, where these carbon NMs altered the structure of the community, as depicted by high throughput sequencing outcomes (Hao et al., 2018). ZnO was reported to hinder the activity soil enzymes like peroxidase, protease and catalase, indicating that not only the NPs, but also the products, formed as a result of their soil interactions, are hazardous to the soil ecosystem (Dinesh et al., 2012). The microbial biomass as well as abundance was found to be retarded as a result of ZnO and TiO2, when they were applied on the soil microbiota (Ge et al., 2011). However, such negative effects of these NMs vary with different engineered nanomaterial (ENM) types and dose, size of the particle, time of contact and exposure profiles. Furthermore, with elevation in the time of contact between soil particles and ENMs, the extent of aging of the soil particles is also elevating parallelly, which makes it difficult to suggest, whether the ENMs incorporated or their aging products are responsible for the hazardous effects (Wu et al., 2019).

Several mechanisms explaining the toxic impact of ENMs, on microorganisms in the soil are yet to be investigated, however, certain possibilities might be disruption of cell membrane and cell walls, damage to the cellular organelles, production of ROS, augmenting oxidative stress, disrupted metabolism, blockage of protein-gene expression etc. (Xie et al., 2017; Lefevre et al., 2016). The major concern is ROS production, as it induces alterations in the cell organelles, damaging the in vivo metabolomic processes, causing death of the cell (Qiana et al., 2020). For instance, ion-transporter proteins, as well as lipopolysaccharides are denatured, as a result of strong reducibility of nZVI, which results in disruption of permeability across the membrane, facilitating entry of ferrous (Fe2+) ions into the cell, which further generate ROS, after reaction with mitochondria-produced hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). This in turn induced oxidative stress and causes cell death (Chen et al., 2011). The reaction between nZVI and lipoteichoic acid, which is the cell wall component of gram-positive bacteria, results in the formation of a complex, as a result of which iron oxide is precipitated, which hinders the functional pores, existing on the cell membrane, thereby blocking nutrient absorption, resulting in cell death (Sacca et al., 2014). However, in the cell walls of multiple fungal species, such alterations in structure, ameliorated growth and cell death, were not observed, even after direct coating of iron precipitates on the cells of the fungi, which thereby indicated presence of chitin in the fungal cell wall, that functions as a protective barrier towards the reductive impact of Nzvi (Otero-Gonzalez et al., 2013). Furthermore, certain investigations have indicated the potential effects of ENMs on spring tails, collembolans, earthworms and other invertebrates present in the soil (Li et al., 2019b; Tatsi et al., 2018), where certain outcomes have shown damaging effects of nZVI on earthworms, when the concentration of nZVI crosses 500 mg/kg in soil. On the other hand, the oxidation and aging products of nZVI depicted lesser toxicity comparatively (El-Temsah and Joner, 2012). In the body of an earthworm, excessive ROS production, resulting in oxidative stress generation, is a possible mechanism that explains the in-vivo toxicity mediated by zinc oxide (Qiana et al., 2020). Through routes, like inhalation, dermal, contact and ingestion, harmful impact on human health has been reported, on exposure to ENMs (Qiana et al., 2020). These substances result in damage to the body organs, and induction of diseases, resulting in genetic and cellular effects, as well as biochemical alterations, when they enter the functioning organs and tissues of the human body (Patil et al., 2016). Therefore, more investigations regarding the risk evaluation procedures and agricultural field monitoring, along with management procedures are required, in order to control the significant health risks (Qiana et al., 2020). On account of minute size and capacity of absorption, elevating soil porosity and interaction with organic substances and soil particles, the ENMs also influence the geophysical and chemical properties of the soil, despite generating significant impact on flora and fauna, as well as human health (Bayat et al., 2019). A 3D fluorescens spectroscopy was carried out to assess the variations in the soil, in the presence and absence of metal oxide NMs, primarily, ferric oxide (Fe3 O4) and CuO, where the humic matter in soil was relatively altered by ENMs, instead of the total organic content (Qiana et al., 2020). A significant increase in the soil micro-porosity, along with stabilization of soil particles and elevated saturated hydraulic conductivity was observed, as a result of administration of magnesium nano-oxide, which altered the physical and mechanical features of the soil (Bayat et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Co-exposure of nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: An investigation was conducted to study the toxic effects of ZnO and Cu in combination, on lettuce, where the outcomes portrayed that in water, the concentration of these two substances, either in dissolved or suspended state, is greatly essential during the assessment of their impact on terrestrial plants, where zinc oxide was reported to curb the toxic profile of copper on the growth of plant root at non-toxic concentration (Qiana et al., 2020). On the other hand, the toxicity, mediated by ZnO NPs was not affected by Cu. The inhibition of zinc channels and blockage of zinc importer proteins, mediated by copper, was referred to the one-sided antagonistic impact in the nano-mixture of copper and zinc oxide (Liu et al., 2016). Significant impact is generated on the soil ecosystem by the ENMs, after interaction with the co-existing contaminants in the soil, which resulted in combined toxicity, varying profiles bioaccumulation and more intense overall effects. The mixture toxicity on plants was reported to be elevated by TiO2, in combination with parabens, like benzyl paraben and methyl and propyl parabens (Soler de la Vega et al., 2019). The complex interactions between conventional pollutants and ENMs were studied, along with their antagonistic, synergistic and additive toxicity profile, where ENMs alleviated the bioaccumulation or environmental toxicity of these pollutants towards animals, microbes, plants and cells (Qiana et al., 2020). For example, the iron oxide NPs retarded the cadmium (Cd2+) and chromium (Cr6+) mediated inhibition of the root growth on wheat, tomato, lettuce and carrot (Hussain et al., 2019, Lopez-Luna et al., 2016). However, the abundance and conformation of bacterial communities in soil containing copper, was not altered by TiO2 addition, but inhibition of activity of enzyme, mediated by Cu2+ was exacerbated by these particles (Fan et al., 2016). Certain investigations also inferred the impact of ENMs on the soil organisms, health of the humans and terrestrial plants, up on entering the underground ecosystem. Table 4 summarizes the toxic actions mediated by multiple nano-formulations on plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanotechnology: priming the pump in sustainable renewable energy
ce:section.ce:para: The use of solar energy, via photovoltaic technology, has been recognized to facilitate eco-sustainable and economical energy production, which has been identified as a significant success of nanotechnology (Rai et al., 2018). On account of their large surface are to volume ratio, the NMs have been identified to positively aid the process of generation of solar anergy and storage of hydrogen fuel and electricity (Rai et al., 2018). The photovoltaic technology has been found to be aided by nanocrystals, nanorods and TiO2 nanotubes, which has exhibited appreciable splitting characteristics of water, inducing benefits in hydrogen production (Chen et al., 2012). Hydrogen energy is going to be the cleanest energy source in the future, while in the present scenario solar energy forms the ultimate energy source, where NPs and NMs play a significant role in augmenting the two (Rai et al., 2018). The solar energy employs the use of photovoltaic technology, which is well-assisted by nanotechnology, resulting in regulation of energy band gap, elevated interchangeability and flexibility, along with the production of an effective optical path, ameliorating the probability of charge recombination (Serrano et al., 2009). The significance of polymeric, nanophase and molecular materials was discussed, in the development of photovoltaic technology, in the review of Royal Society of Chemistry. Also, a third energy international conference was held in Tandem, entitled, “Nanotechnologies for Solar and Thermal Energy Conversion and Storage”, which was critically significant (Rai et al., 2018). The photovoltaic and photocatalytic applications of quantum dots (2–10 nm) have also been explored, on account of their unique characteristics. An energy effective nanocomposite, CdTe/NTU-9, was developed for more effective trapping of solar energy, unlike the conventional semi-conductors (Kaur et al., 2017).

The techno-economic revolution has led to the improved energy paradigm, by the introduction of nanotechnology-associated solar cells, based up on graphene and quantum dots. NMs have significantly aided in solar energy production (Rai et al., 2018). For instance, the optical selectivity has been improved by nanofluids, owing to their minute size and direct effective solar energy absorption, resulting in more heat transfer (Taylor et al., 2011). Quantum dots have been recognized as efficient emitters of light, on account of their ability to release numerous electrons per solar photon, with varying emission and absorption spectra, based upon the size of the particles. the solar cells, based upon silicon, have been replaced by a combination of dye-sensitized colloidal TiO2 films and NPs, due to low cost (Rai et al., 2018). Also, conductive polymers, associated with NPs, based on metal oxides, has resulted in the formation of nanocomposites, to facilitate solar energy trapping (Serrano et al., 2009). The solar energy sector was reformed by graphene (2D NPs, based up on carbon) by its impact on the nano-based solar cells, due to its efficient chemical characteristics (elevated thermal and electrical conductivity, increased surface area, boosted mechanical flexibility) and hexagonal geometry (Rai et al., 2018). Uneconomical platinum has been replaced by graphene sheets, containing nitrogen, to be applied as catalysts for redox reactions in fuel cells (Novoselov et al., 2004; Jiang et al., 2011; Hu et al., 2015). Structurally modified graphene was found to be compatible with polypyrrole and polyaniline, which are economical, eco-stable conducting polymers, which resulted in effective storage and energy conservation (Cong et al., 2013). The combination of MWCNTs and graphene nanoribbons, was conjugated with CNTs and graphene sheets, exhibiting appreciable energy storage. Also, graphene functioned as effective electron acceptor in solar cells of cadmium sulphide (CdS) quantum dots, unlike SWCNTs (Li et al., 2011). As effective electron acceptor, graphene quantum dots exhibit appreciable electron mobility and energy production (Gupta et al., 2011). Nanofabricated graphene has been used as capacitors as well as fuel cells, to capture solar radiation. Also, the combination of graphene and piezoelectric materials harvests tremendous amount of energy, as portrayed by Pb–Zr–Ti ribbons and ZnO NMs (Kwon et al., 2012; S.-Y. Lee et al., 2013). By using NPs like, CdS, TiO2, silicon carbide (SiC), and so on, nanotechnology has facilitated the environment-friendly and economic production of hydrogen from solar energy, via photocatalytic splitting of water (Ni et al., 2007). Nickel (Ni) NPs – modified porous carbon nanofibers exhibit appreciable efficacy in storage of hydrogen. Nanostructures of CNTs aid in hydrogen storage/sorption on activated carbon (SWCNTs and MWCNTs), as well as graphene nanofibers, via stability of chemical bond (Nikitin et al., 2008). The combination of CNTs and fuel cells, based up on polymer electrolyte, exhibits potential results in hydrogen storage. The CNTs also enhance the performance of polymer-electrolyte based fuel cells and abbreviate fuel cell cost, along with improvement in mechanical strength of the cells, via acting as reinforcing fillers (Rai et al., 2018). The large surface area and elevated thermal conductivity, due to availability of graphene tubular structures, also accounts for their optimum role as electrocatalysts (Zhang et al., 2011). Additionally, 3D titania nanotubes encapsulated by palladium NPs, form an electrode material, which is an energy-effective source for electrolysis of biomass, concerning to hydrogen economy (Chen et al., 2014). Furthermore, the organo-metallic framework generated by coordination polymers and nanotubes, may contribute to storage of hydrogen (Collins and Zhou, 2007). The emission of air pollutants was retarded when Cerium oxide NPs were used as additives in the biodiesel fuel (Sajith et al., 2010). The combustion of diesel fuel is curbed by nanoformulation of aluminium (Al) NPs and nanofluids. Doping of CNTs with metal hydrides has reported elevated hydrogen storage. The sensors, based up on CNTs have exhibited potential role in green nanocomposites and environmental pollutant remediation (Rai et al., 2018). The loss of electricity during distribution and transmission of power was curbed by nanowires, along with amelioration of thermal and cooling energy losses in industrial sectors (Rahman et al., 2016; Tan et al., 2012). The use of advanced membrane NMs has further minimized the desalination energy (Tan et al., 2012).

The magnetic NPs also upgrade the energy sector. The biodiesel is prepared by nanomagnetic catalyst KF/Cao-Fe3O4, which exerted a significant impact on its development (Hu et al., 2011). The biodiesel production is efficiently catalyzed by sulfated zirconia NPs and sodium titanate nanotubes (Chen et al., 2013; Hernández-Hipólito et al., 2014). NPs also facilitate rapid production of ethanol and hydrogen, via fermentation, in water hyacinth, which is capable of NP phytoremediation (Zada et al., 2013). Furthermore, the production of wind energy is elevated by nanocolloidal boron nitride-based lubricants, by increasing the efficiency of wind turbines (Nano-connect, 2017). Moreover, the field of nanotechnology exhibits potential role in providing clean water, via magnetic, anti-microbial and nanophotocatalytic properties, along with solar disinfection (Qu et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Future prospects
ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology has exhibited a potential impact on the agricultural field, however, the methods and approaches in order to handle potential hazards caused by them are not known by the general population, thereby imposing significant environmental and human health risks. Therefore, planned research should be carried out, along with proper formulation policies, extracted from appropriate scientific data. The NP-centered research in the future, should focus on attaining comprehensive knowledge on unrecognized NPs generated by mycorrhiza and root endophytes, which might be able to produce optimized role in plant productivity; strengthening NP controlled synthesis; evaluating the non-target impact of NPs; production, characterization and role of NPs, produced biologically, for economical use; nanosensor development, depending upon pesticide, water, plant and soil; interaction of NPs with plants, as well as transport mechanism of NPs inside plant; critical evaluation of NP side effects on multiple environmental aspects; advanced approaches for seed coating, with NP, enriched with micronutrients, for customized production of seeds; and return of investment (ROI) calculation for nanobiotechnology-based agriculture on field trials and pilot scale (Acharya and Pal, 2020). Further, expert institutions are required to evaluate the biosafety of NPs and documentation of relevant scientific data. Proper guidelines should be framed related to NP evaluation and monitoring, as per the WHO and FAO standards (Sharma and Nimesh, 2017). Assessment of food products, based upon nanotechnology, should be done, prior to their commercial release. The toxic impact of NPs on the marine life should be investigated and documented. In order to minimize the issues related to the agricultural field, certified and skilled manpower should be hired. The economic aid towards the applications of nanotechnology should be strengthened, and the partnership between the public and private firms should aid in proper funding and research (Acharya and Pal, 2020). More awareness should be spread among the general population, regarding the significance of nanotechnology. Furthermore, there should be constant interaction between the environmentalists, Botanists and nano-researchers, as well as the general population, related to the advantages and limitations associated with the use nanotechnology (Rai et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology has been employed to heighten the field of agriculture, and minimize the problems related to it. The concept of nanoformulations has revolutionized the farming practices, with effective and reliable outcomes, as well as limited side effects, environmental, toxicity and biotic hazards. The authors envisage, that both the risks and benefits associated with the use of nanotechnology must be equitably judged, in order take effective decisions, in compliance with the agricultural practices. Nanotechnology is an emerging field, which is open to a more extensive perspective, but till now the applications of nano-farming and NP-associated agricultural practices dominate the negative aspects, to some extent, which promotes the use of this technology in the present-day farming and employing this technology to enhance the agricultural outcome. However, it doesn't abbreviate the negative response of this technology towards plants, which should, at no cost, be neglected. The prime aim to investigate the drawbacks, associated with this technology, is to understand the risks associated with it, which would further elevate its agricultural reliability as well as aid in providing suitable solutions, and in order to abide by the concept of “Technology-yes, but safety-must”, the global researchers, environmentalists and Botanists need to consider both the pros and cons of implementing nanotechnology in the agricultural paradigm.
